items.question:
A 67-year-old man with no notable medical history is referred for evaluation of symptoms suspicious for hypoglycemia. These symptoms occur when the patient avoids calories for a period exceeding 2 to 3 hours and are characterized by sweating, tremor, and tachycardia. He has been waking up 3 times each night to consume a snack, and he carries orange juice with him constantly. These symptoms have been present to some degree for about 4 years, but have worsened in the past 6 months. Over the 4-year period, he has gained approximately 100 lb (45.5 kg). His current BMI is 43 kg/m².

On the basis of this history, his primary care physician ordered CT of the abdomen, which suggested the presence of a discrete mass in the head of the pancreas. At the time of his initial evaluation, the patient was asymptomatic after a 2-hour fast. However, the following laboratory values were obtained:

 $$ \mathrm{Glucose}=50\mathrm{mg}/\mathrm{dL}(2.78\mathrm{mmol/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Proinsulin}=1587\mathrm{pg}/\mathrm{mL}(180\mathrm{pmol/L}) $$ 

 $$  C-peptide=14.3\ ng/mL(4.7nmol/L) $$ 

Insulin antibodies, undetectable

 $$  Insulin=112\ \mu IU/mL\ (777.8\ pmol/L) $$ 

Sulfonylurea screen (including repaglinide and glimepiride), negative

Review of the abdominal CT performed at an outside institution documents the presence of a 2.4-cm enhancing mass in the head of the pancreas with central inhomogeneity and areas of low attenuation. The appearance is consistent with an islet-cell tumor of the pancreas. No other masses are seen, but the mass is encasing the pancreatic duct. The pancreas and the liver are also characterized by extensive fatty infiltration.

Which of the following describes appropriate management?
options: Initiate chemotherapy
Recommend a weight-loss program before considering surgery
Refer the patient for ethanol ablation under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography
Ask an experienced endocrine surgeon to enucleate the tumor
Initiate verapamil
items.explanation:
3 ANSWER: C) Refer the patient for ethanol ablation under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography. A typical approach to insulinoma would be to consider enucleation and, in normal circumstances, Answer D would be correct. However, there are several unusual features of this patient's presentation. Typically, insulinoma does not cause an elevation in β-cell polypeptides that is as marked as that observed in this vignette and elevations of such magnitude should raise the possibility that hypoglycemia is caused by insulin antibodies. However, this possibility is excluded by undetectable insulin antibodies. There is a strong possibility that this insulinoma is malignant given its size, behavior, and inhomogeneous appearance. Their relatively slow growth often makes chemotherapy ineffective and in the absence of a tissue diagnosis, initiating chemotherapy (Answer A) is incorrect. The proximity to the main pancreatic duct and the location in the head strongly suggests that enucleation (Answer D) is not technically possible. Moreover, a Whipple procedure or any pancreatic procedure in the presence of fat infiltration places the patient at high risk of complications such as pancreatic fistula or anastomotic breakdown.

A staged approach was taken in this case, using intervention to decrease unregulated insulin secretion from the insulinoma and to permit weight loss before definitive surgery. Diazoxide and somatostatin were not viable options in this patient, primarily because of cost and, in the case of somatostatin, the patient was unwilling to sell inject 3 times daily. Given some previous success with ethanol ablation (Answer C), this approach was undertaken with subsequent short-term benefit in that the patient was able to fast for periods of 4 to 6 hours without clinically significant symptoms. He was also able to avoid nocturnal snacking and this led to some weight loss (~20 lb [9.1 kg]). Ethanol injection is performed under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography. Typically between 0.5 and 1.0 mL are injected and, especially for large tumors, more than one treatment session is required.

Weight loss (Answer B) may improve the ease with which pancreaticoduodenectomy proceeds and may decrease the risk of operative complications. However, impairing this patient's ability to eat and using caloric intake to treat or prevent hypoglycemia is not the best way forward.

Several case reports suggest that verapamil (Answer E) may help prevent hypoglycemia in patients with insulinoma. Many patients referred to tertiary care centers for the treatment of insulinoma have been prescribed this medication with poor results necessitating surgical treatment.

##### Educational Objective:

Evaluate management options for a large insulinoma in the head of the pancreas.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Insulinoma

##### Reference(s):

Gaujoux S, Cortes A, Couvelard A, et al. Fatty pancreas and increased body mass index are risk factors of pancreatic fistula after pancreaticoduodenectomy. Surgery. 2010;148(1):15-23.

Rosso E, Casnedi S, Pessaux P, et al. The role of "fatty pancreas" and of BMI in the occurrence of pancreatic fistula after pancreaticoduodenectomy. J Gastrointest Surg. 2009;13(10):1845-1851.

Levy MJ, Thompson GB, Topazian MD, Callstrom MR, Grant CS, Vella A. US-guided ethanol ablation of insulinomas: a new treatment option. Gastrointest Endosc. 2012;75(1):200-206.

Cryer PE, Axelrod L, Grossman AB, et al; Endocrine Society. Evaluation and management of adult hypoglycemic disorders: an Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(3):709-728.

ANSWER: C) Statin

Although he is young, this patient has several cardiovascular disease risk factors including type 1 diabetes, cigarette smoking, positive family history, and dyslipidemia (elevated LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, low HDL cholesterol). items.question:
A 49-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus of 18 years' duration, hyperlipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and obesity presents for steadily worsening nausea and abdominal pain over the last 72 hours without any obvious exacerbating factors. The pain is primarily epigastric in location and seems worse with eating, although it has become more constant in the past day. He has started to run a low-grade fever (101°F [38.3°C]) and has had 2 episodes of nonbloody bilious emesis in the past 24 hours.

He currently follows an insulin therapy regimen with insulin detemir, 60 units twice daily, and insulin aspart, 10 units before each meal. He takes simvastatin for elevated LDL cholesterol diagnosed 2 years ago and lansoprazole to manage gastroesophageal reflux disease. He typically consumes 2 to 3 alcoholic beverages per day but has not done so since the onset of abdominal pain. In recent weeks, he has been frequently consuming fast-food meals and, as in the past, has occasionally been nonadherent with his insulin therapy. His blood glucose values over the past 72 hours ranged from 220 to 350 mg/dL (12.21 to 19.43 mmol/L).

On physical examination, he has central obesity, but no evidence of abdominal striae. Weight is 190 lb (86.4 kg), and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 31.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 135/95 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 76 beats/min. He has acanthosis nigricans. The thyroid gland is normal without nodularity. Abdominal examination reveals marked epigastric and right upper quadrant tenderness without rebound but with some guarding. Bowel sounds are diminished. No masses are identified. He has a rash consisting of reddish-yellow lesions on the backs of both legs, on the buttocks, and on his knees. The retinal blood vessels show a creamy appearance on fundoscopic examination.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} = 13.5\% $ (0.135)

Creatinine = 0.8 mg/dL (70.7 μmol/L)

Sodium = 128 mEq/L (128 mmol/L)

Potassium = 4.5 mEq/L (4.5 mmol/L)

White blood cell count = 28,500/μL (25.5 × 10⁹/L) with a left shift TSH = 1.5 mIU/L

Which of the following is the most likely cause of his current symptoms?
options: Gastroparesis
Pancreatitis
Ulcerative colitis
Cholecystitis
Mesenteric ischemia
items.explanation:
7 ANSWER: B) Pancreatitis

Acute pancreatitis (Answer B) is characterized by inflammation of the pancreas with extension of the inflammatory response to other abdominal sites. The severity of acute pancreatitis is variable—mild-to-moderate abdominal pain, pancreatic necrosis, and even catastrophic abdominal inflammation and death. The cause incidence of acute pancreatitis ranges from 4.9 to 35 per 100,000 persons, and it is a leading cause of hospitalization in the United States. Numerous factors predispose an individual to acute pancreatitis including any disease/obstruction, alcohol, hypercalcemia, and hypertriglyceridemia (as was the case in this vignette). Uncontrolled hyperglycemia in the setting of diabetes promotes the risk of hypertriglyceridemia-induced creatitis. Insulin, along with thyroid hormone, is an important regulator of lipoprotein lipase activity, a key one involved in the processing of triglyceride-rich particles including chylomicrons and VLDL. Either insurance or insulin deficiency reduces lipoprotein lipase activity and results in hypertriglyceridemia. Triglycerin�ations of 1000 mg/dL or higher (≥11.30 mmol/L) increase the risk of acute pancreatitis, although closely lower levels do not confer protection. Children with inherited disorders of lipoprotein metabolism increased risk of hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis. Patients with pancreatitis associated with glyceridemia usually present with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Typical findings on physical include eruptive xanthomas over the extensor surfaces of the extremities, buttocks, and back; lipemia and hepatosplenomegaly from fatty infiltration of the liver.

enteric ischemia (Answer E) results from decreased blood flow through the intestinal vasculature. Chronic ischemia may present with “intestinal angina” and is characterized by recurrent acute episodes of pain during periods of heightened intestinal demand, such as after oral intake. Affected patients can crampy, postprandial epigastric pain, usually within an hour of eating. Subsequently, the pain subsides.

Some affected patients develop a food aversion due to the association with pain and subsequently eight loss. Patients with foregut ischemia from celiac artery insufficiency may have nausea, vomiting.

Cholecystitis (Answer D) is an acute inflammation of the gall bladder. Symptoms usually include steady, worsening right upper quadrant pain with associated guarding, fever, and nausea. Affected patients usually have leukocytosis and a positive Murphy sign, which is associated with increased pain on palpation of the gall bladder, and patients catching their breath during inspiration. Such patients are usually ill appearing and lay still because the pain is exacerbated by movement. The episode of pain lasts for several hours and is often aggravated by consumption of a fatty meal.

Gastroparesis (Answer A) is an autonomic complication in patients with diabetes and it is associated with nausea and early satiety. Affected patients are unlikely to present with abdominal pain.

Inflammatory bowel disease, including ulcerative colitis (Answer C), occurs at a higher incidence in patients with other autoimmune conditions. Like pancreatitis, ulcerative colitis can present with acute abdominal pain and signs of inflammation or infection. Usually there is a history of bloody diarrhea, or at least very frequent stools with mucus, which would not be the case in acute pancreatitis. The classic rash associated with ulcerative colitis is pyoderma gangrenosa, an ulcerating rash that tends to spread slowly.

##### Educational Objective:

Diagnose hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis in a patient with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Hypertriglyceridemia-induced acute pancreatitis

##### Reference(s):

Yadav D, Agarwal N, Pitchumoni CS. A critical evaluation of laboratory tests in acute pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2002;97(6):1309-1318.

Fortson MR, Freedman SN, Web

items.question:
A 65-year-old man with a 30-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus returns to see you for a routine follow-up appointment. He was referred to you for management 2 years ago after recurrent episodes of hypoglycemia, some of which had been severe and required the assistance of others. At that time, his insulin therapy was changed to a regimen including once-daily dosing of insulin glargine and multiple daily doses of insulin aspart with meals.

The patient keeps a careful blood glucose log in which he records 8 to 10 fingerstick blood glucose readings daily. His glycemic control stabilized following the change in his insulin regimen. Although he periodically experiences blood glucose levels between 60 and 70 mg/dL (3.33-3.89 mmol/L) in the very early morning, his ability to sense hypoglycemia has improved. The patient's most recent episode of severe hypoglycemia occurred 18 months ago during the nighttime while he was asleep. His diabetes is complicated by microalbuminuria, peripheral neuropathy, and retinopathy that has required photocoagulation but not resulted in clinically significant vision impairment.

Although he has never experienced a driving mishap related to hypoglycemia, he voluntarily stopped driving when he was referred to you for care. He now asks whether it would be advisable for him to resume operation of a motor vehicle for personal use.

On physical examination, the patient is alert and appears well. Blood pressure is 127/64 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 62 beats/min. Height is 67.5 in (171.5 cm), and weight is 162 lb (73.6 kg) (BMI = 25.0 kg/m²). He has evidence of previous photocoagulation on undilated fundoscopic examination, but visual fields are grossly intact. He has no foot deformities, foot lesions, or gait abnormalities. He has a few areas of sensory loss on monofilament testing of the plantar surfaces of both feet, but proprioception is preserved.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=7.6\% $ (0.076)

Sodium = 141 mEq/L (141 mmol/L)

Glomerular filtration rate = 70.2 mL/min per 1.73 m²

Creatinine = 1.3 mg/dL (114.9 μmol/L)

TSH = 1.85 mIU/L

In addition to reducing the patient's dosage of insulin glargine, which of the following should you recommend now?
options: Continued avoidance of driving given his high risk of hypoglycemia-related mishaps
Continued avoidance of driving given his diabetes-related complications
Resumption of driving after education to minimize risk of hypoglycemia while driving
Transition to insulin pump therapy before resumption of driving
Use of a continuous glucose-monitoring device during all periods of driving
items.explanation:
9 ANSWER: C) Resumption of driving after education to minimize risk of hypoglycemia while driving. Although most patients with diabetes are able to safely operate a motor vehicle, care must be taken to ideal those patients at high risk for driving-related mishaps. Patients with a history of hypoglycemia, impaired vision, peripheral neuropathy may be at increased risk for such events; however, the presence of these conditions should not automatically disqualify a patient from driving. Personalized assessment of driving safety and appropriate counseling and care to minimize the risk of hypoglycemia are essential to reducing the risk of motor vehicle accidents. Patients with hypoglycemia that occur without explanation or warning and those with a history of severe hypoglycemia within the past year are considered to be at higher risk; however, hypoglycemia that has occurred only during sleep should not automatically result in driving restrictions. Notably, the most significant predictor of collisions appears to be a history of hypoglycemia and/or mishaps occurring while driving.

This patient with longstanding type 1 diabetes does have a history of severe hypoglycemia; however, this is not a problem since his insulin regimen was modified. He is measuring his fingerstick blood glucose level many times per day, and he has not documented frequent problems with hypoglycemia. His last episode of sev hypoglycemia occurred 18 months ago and was not during daytime hours. Most importantly, he has no history driving mishaps and has only voluntarily discontinued use of his motor vehicle. Continued avoidance of driving because of fear of hypoglycemia-related collision (Answer A) does not appear necessary. As previously noted, there are particular diabetes-related complications that may pose a contraindication to driving. However, as this patient does not have clinically significant vision impairment and has only mild sensory loss in his feet, he does need to avoid driving because of his complications (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

This patient should resume use of his vehicle after receiving appropriate education and counseling to minimize the likelihood of hypoglycemia while driving (Answer C). The American Diabetes Association recommends that the counseling include instructions to always check blood glucose before driving and regularly thereafter if an individual is driving for longer than 1 hour. Education regarding the impact of even modest hypoglycemia on driving and decision-making should be included in this counseling. Should hypoglycemia occur during operation of a vehicle, driving should immediately cease and not resume until 30 to 60 minutes after the low blood glucose is adequately treated to ensure full recovery of neurocognitive function. Drivers should always have access to a blood glucose and snacks, including a form of rapid-acting glucose, in the vehicle. This patient should be regularly reassessed at follow-up appointments regarding his ability to continue to drive safely. If hypoglycemia becomes a more frequent significant problem, or if his diabetes-related complications progress, he may need to relinquish his driving privilege.

Although this patient may be a candidate for insulin pump therapy for other reasons, this change in therapy would not be required before motor vehicle use. He is currently experiencing little to no hypoglycemia on his regimen of multiple daily insulin injections, and thus a change to insulin pump therapy (Answer D) would not enhance his driving safety. Use of a continuous glucose-monitoring device during periods of driving may be a reasonable consideration for some patients. This patient monitors his blood glucose levels very closely and is not currently experiencing frequent or unexplained hypoglycemia; thus, a requirement that he use continuous glucose monitoring while driving (Answer E) is unnecessary. Furthermore, use of a continuous glucose-monitoring device would not be an appropriate substi

items.question:
You are asked to help care for a 39-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus who presented to the emergency department by ambulance for increasing lethargy and altered mental status. She is unable to provide any. Her daughters report that she has had abdominal pain for the past 2 days and that she began vomiting 2 before she became less responsive at home. She has had diabetes for 24 years, which has been complicated heranuropathy and retinopathy. She uses an insulin pump. Her hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level was 8.4% (0.084) before today's presentation. In the past 6 months, she has been admitted to the hospital twice.

On physical examination, her temperature is  $ 97^{\circ} $F ( $ 36.1^{\circ} $C), supine blood pressure is 92/61 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 124 beats/min. She has deep respirations at a rate of 28 breaths/min. Her breath has a fruity odor. She is oriented only to person, speaks in broken sentences, and is confused. There are dry oral mucus membranes and no pharyngeal injection or exudates. Her lungs are clear. There are no murmurs on cardiac examination. Her abdomen is soft, moderately tender throughout, and nondistended. She has decreased skin turgor. Her insulin pump infusion set is located on her left lower abdomen, with no erythema or discharge. The insulin pump's battery is not functioning.

Laboratory test results:

 $$ \mathrm{Venous~pH}=6.96 $$ 

 $$  Glucose=999\ mg/dL(55.44\ mmol/L) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Lactic~acid}=57.8\mathrm{mg/dL}(6.3\mathrm{mmol/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Sodium}=129\mathrm{m E q/L}(129\mathrm{mmol/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{P o t a s s i u m}=5.6\mathrm{m E q/L}(5.6\mathrm{m o m o l/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{White~blood~cell~count}=33,140/\mu\mathrm{L}(33.14\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{CO}_{2}=8\mathrm{mEq}/\mathrm{L}(8\mathrm{mmol}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Anion~gap}=36\mathrm{mEq/L} $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Platelet\ count}=165\times10^{3}/\mu\mathrm{L}(165\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

Creatinine = 2.1 mg/dL (185.6 μmol/L)

Urine toxicology screen, negative

Plasma osmolality = 359 mOsm/kg (359 mmol/kg) (5 hours after presentation)

#### In addition to administration of intravenous isotonic saline, which of the following actions will ensure the best outcome in this patient?
options: Modify insulin treatment on the basis of serial arterial pH
Administer sodium bicarbonate together with insulin
Once blood glucose falls below 200 mg/dL (11.10 mmol/L), add 5% dextrose to the intravenous fluids and continue intravenous insulin until the acidosis has resolved
Use intravenous insulin analogues rather than human regular insulin
Administer a subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin analogue every 2 hours
items.explanation:
## 16 ANSWER: C) Once blood glucose falls below 200 mg/dL (11.10 mmol/L), add 5% dextrose to the intravenous fluids and continue intravenous insulin until the acidosis has resolved intravenous fluid Diabetic ketoacidosis is a life-threatening complication of severe insulin deficiency that is commonly seen in patients with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes. However, it can also occur in patients with type 1 diabetes who interrupt basal and bolus insulin for more than 24 hours, or in those on insulin pump therapy whose rapid-acting insulin analogue infusion is interrupted for more than 4 to 6 hours. Diabetic ketoacidosis may also complicate severe stressful illness in patients with type 1 or type 2 diabetes. The severity of ketoacidosis is determined on the basis of the arterial pH, serum bicarbonate, and alteration of sensorium:
MeasurementSeverity of Ketoacidosis
MildModerateSevere
Arterial pH7.25-7.307.00-7.24<7.00
Serum bicarbonate15-18 mEq/L\n(15-18 mmol/L)10-14 mEq/L\n(10-14 mmol/L)<10 mEq/L\n(<10 mmol/L)
Alteration in sensoriumAlertAlert/drowsyStupor/coma
The standard of treatment of severe diabetic ketoacidosis involves aggressive fluid resuscitation; intravenous insulin; repletion of potassium; and close monitoring of hydration status, glucose, acidosis, and electrolytes. Following serum carbon dioxide or venous pH has mostly replaced following the arterial pH (Answer A) to assess adequacy of therapy. This avoids the pain and potential complications of serial arterial blood gas determination. Venous pH is about 0.03 units lower than arterial pH, a nonclinically significant difference. Insulin therapy is the primary intervention to correct acidosis and it acts by inducing the metabolism of the ketoacids $ \beta $-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate to bicarbonate. Insulin directly inhibits lipolysis and ketogenesis, promotes clearance of free fatty acids, and inhibits fatty acid oxidation to ketones in the liver. Administration of bicarbonate (Answer B) to patients with diabetic ketoacidosis who present with a pH as low as 6.9 has not been shown to speed correction of acidosis or reduce mortality. No randomized controlled studies of bicarbonate use have been performed in patients with a pH below 6.9. In those with marked hyperkalemia complicating ketoacidosis, bicarbonate can be used to accelerate the intracellular translocation of potassium from the intravascular space. When treating ketoacidosis, intravenous insulin must be continued until the acidosis has cleared. To prevent hypoglycemia from occurring with continued intravenous insulin infusion, it is recommended that 5% dextrose be added to intravenous fluids when glucose levels fall below 200 mg/dL (<11.10 mmol/L) (thus, Answer C is correct). Normalization of acidosis, ketosis, and hyperglycemia is similar when intravenous regular insulin is compared with intravenous rapid-acting insulin (thus, Answer D is incorrect). I
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 16: "Treat advanced diabetic ketoacidosis."

## 16 ANSWER: C) Once blood glucose falls below 200 mg/dL (11.10 mmol/L), add 5% dextrose to the intravenous fluids and continue intravenous insulin until the acidosis has resolved intravenous fluid Diabetic ketoacidosis is a life-threatening complication of severe insulin deficiency that is commonly seen in patients with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes. However, it can also occur in patients with type 1 diabetes who interrupt basal and bolus insulin for more than 24 hours, or in those on insulin pump therapy whose rapid-acting insulin analogue infusion is interrupted for more than 4 to 6 hours. Diabetic ketoacidosis may also complicate severe stressful illness in patients with type 1 or type 2 diabetes. The severity of ketoacidosis is determined on the basis of the arterial pH, serum bicarbonate, and alteration of sensorium:
MeasurementSeverity of Ketoacidosis
MildModerateSevere
Arterial pH7.25-7.307.00-7.24<7.00
Serum bicarbonate15-18 mEq/L\n(15-18 mmol/L)10-14 mEq/L\n(10-14 mmol/L)<10 mEq/L\n(<10 mmol/L)
Alteration in sensoriumAlertAlert/drowsyStupor/coma
The standard of treatment of severe diabetic ketoacidosis involves aggressive fluid resuscitation; intravenous insulin; repletion of potassium; and close monitoring of hydration status, glucose, acidosis, and electrolytes. items.question:
A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus presents for a follow-up visit with concerns of weight loss and a declining sense of well-being. Her diabetes is treated with intensive insulin therapy including insulin glargine in combination with insulin lispro. She has always had modest glycemic control with hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ values between 8.0% and 8.5% (0.080 and 0.085) and hyperglycemia. However, she reports unexplained hypoglycemia. Low blood glucose values occur at any time day or night. She has little warning, she does experience hunger, anxiety, and diaphoresis when her glucose levels are low. She has recorded values between 20 and 40 mg/dL (1.11 and 2.22 mmol/L) on numerous occasions in the past 3 weeks. Additionally, expresses concerns over a concurrent 10-lb (4.5-kg) weight loss. She has had no diarrhea, palpitations, tremor temperature intolerance.

On physical examination, weight is 105 lb (47.7 kg) and height is 60.5 in (153.7 cm) (BMI = 20.2 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 95/70 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 96 beats/min. Mucus membranes are moist without lesions. thyroid gland is normal without nodularity. Her reflexes are normal. Findings on cardiac examination are normal. There is no tremor of the outstretched hands.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=7.7\% $ (0.077)

Creatinine = 0.8 mg/dL (70.7 μmol/L)

Sodium = 133 mEq/L (133 mmol/L)

Calcium = 9.4 mg/dL (2.35 mmol/L)

Potassium = 5.8 mEq/L (5.8 mmol/L)

Urinary albumin excretion = 10 mg/24 h

Which of the following diagnostic tests should you order?
options: TSH measurement
Tissue transglutaminase antibody assessment
Cortisol and ACTH measurement
Tryptophan hydroxylase antibody assessment

Estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer was recently diagnosed after a routine mammogram revealed the presence of a breast mass. She underwent bilateral mastectomy 2 months ago, and sentinel lymph node biopsy at the time of surgery was negative. Her oncologist is planning to begin therapy with anastrozole. A recent bone density test revealed a femoral neck T score of -2.2 and a lumbar spine T score of -1.9. She has no history of fractures or height loss. Other than a daily multivitamin, she takes no medications.

items.question:
A 54-year-old woman is referred to you for assistance with type 1 diabetes management. Diabetes was diagnosed at age 44 years. Her current insulin regimen includes insulin glargine, 10 units in the morning and 5 units in the evening. She boluses insulin aspart before meals using an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of 1 unit for each 15 g of carbohydrate and a sensitivity factor of 1 unit to lower her glucose 100 mg/dL (5.55 mmol/L) to a target level of 100 mg/dL (5.55 mmol/L). She participates in aerobic exercises for 60 to 90 minutes daily and weight training for 30 minutes 3 times per week. Her diet is rich in fruits, vegetables, lean meats, and mostly high-fiber carbohydrates. She feels comfortable with carbohydrate counting when eating at home, but has more difficulty accurately assessing the carbohydrate content of foods eaten at restaurants. She measures her glucose level 4 to 8 times daily. Her downloaded glucose meter shows glucose concentrations that range from 42 to 493 mg/dL (2.33 to 27.36 mmol/L) with a mean of 168 mg/dL (9.32 mmol/L) and a standard deviation of 76 mg/dL (4.22 mmol/L). Glucose levels are least variable after eating a similar daily breakfast and are quite variable throughout the rest of the day. She senses that her glucose levels are low when they fall below 60 mg/dL (3.33 mmol/L) because she feels “different.” She has not had any episodes of severe hypoglycemia requiring assistance. She takes atorvastatin, 10 mg daily, for hyperlipidemia and ramipril, 2.5 mg daily, for hypertension.

On physical examination, her blood pressure is 114/69 mm Hg and pulse rate is 72 beats/min. Height is 69 in (175.3 cm), and weight is 133 lb (60.5 kg) (BMI = 19.6 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Findings on eye, cardiovascular, abdominal, and peripheral nerve examinations are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=7.6\% $ (0.076)

LDL cholesterol = 52 mg/dL (1.35 mmol/L)

Urinary albumin = 7 mg/g creatinine

The patient asks whether insulin pump therapy would assist with her diabetes control.

Which of the following outcomes would be most likely if this patient's insulin regimen were changed to insulin pump therapy?
options: Reduced hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $
Reduced flexibility in the timing of her meals
Reduced risk of diabetic ketoacidosis
Increased total daily insulin dose requirement
Increased frequency of severe hypoglycemia
items.explanation:
## 30 ANSWER: A) Reduced hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $

Insulin pump therapy has become an increasingly popular mode of insulin delivery in patients with type 1 diabetes since its successful use in the Diabetes Control and Complications Trial. Benefits of continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII) include adjustable basal insulin dosing, allowing for changes in basal rates depending on time of day and physical activity levels. Insulin pumps also allow use of very small increments of insulin down to 0.05 units, which is useful in highly insulin-sensitive patients, such as the woman presented in this vignette. In clinical trials comparing CSII with multiple daily injections, the benefits of pump therapy are dependent on the types of comparator injectable insulin.

In adults, studies comparing CSII with basal insulin analogue glargine- and lispro-based multiple daily injection regimens predominantly show reductions in hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ with CSII (thus, Answer A is correct), but not in frequency of or severity of hypoglycemia (thus, Answer E is incorrect). In these studies, fasting glucose levels are similar in the 2 groups because of insulin titration protocols targeting a fasting glucose goal. A long-term case-control study in children showed a reduction in both total and severe hypoglycemia in CSII-treated patients vs multiple daily injection-treated patients. A 10% to 20% insulin dosage reduction is commonly recommended when insulin pump therapy is initiated in patients with only mildly elevated hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ levels. Reductions in the total daily insulin dose can be seen in some patients when compared with the dose in multiple daily injection-treated patients, perhaps because of more consistent insulin absorption from a single subcutaneous insulin depot in pump therapy (thus, Answer D is incorrect). Overall, ketoacidosis rates are not higher in patients treated with insulin pump therapy vs multiple daily injections, although ketoacidosis can develop more rapidly in patients treated only with short- or rapid-acting insulin should the insulin infusion be interrupted (thus, Answer C is incorrect).

Properly dosed basal rates using pump therapy or basal glargine or detemir insulins do not precipitate hypoglycemia if a meal is missed or delayed (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

##### Educational Objective:

Counsel a patient with type 1 diabetes mellitus on potential outcomes of switching from a multiple daily injection regimen to insulin pump therapy.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Insulin pump therapy in adults with type 1 diabetes mellitus

##### Reference(s):

Johnson SR, Cooper MN, Jones TW, Davis EA. Long-term outcome of insulin pump therapy in children with type 1 diabetes assessed in a large population-based case-control study. Diabetologia. 2013;56(11):2392-2400.

Pickup JC, Renard E. Long-acting insulin analogs versus insulin pump therapy for the treatment of type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(Suppl 2):S140-S145.

Doyle EA, Weinzimer SA, Steffen AT, Ahern JA, Vincent M, Tamborlane WV. A randomized, prospective trial comparing the efficacy of continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion with multiple daily injections using insulin glargine. Diabetes Care. 2004;27(7):1554-1558.

items.question:
A 64-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus returns to see you for an appointment to reassess management of his peripheral neuropathy. He has managed his diabetes with the use of an insulin pump for the past 8 years and has had no notable problems related to the use of the device. He monitors his fingerstick blood glucose levels 6 to 8 times daily and keeping a carefully documented log of the results. Review of his log reveals that he has occasional nonsevere hypoglycemia during periods of prolonged exertion. He also has blood glucose readings above 200 mg/dL (11.10 mmol/L) 2 to 3 hours after his evening meal several times each week.

The patient's diabetes is complicated by painful peripheral neuropathy. He describes stinging and burning sensations in both lower legs and feet. The discomfort is interfering with his sleep because it is more noticeable at night and is aggravated by touch from sheets or blankets. Three months ago, he began pregabalin at a dosage of 50 mg orally 3 times daily, which he increased to 100 mg 3 times daily after 1 week. He has tolerated the medication well and reports good adherence to therapy; however, he has had no noticeable improvement in his neuropathic symptoms.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 80 beats/min. Height is 74 in (188 cm), and weight is 208 lb (94.5 kg) (BMI = 26.7 kg/m $ ^{2} $). He has no obvious foot deformities or foot ulcerations, and his ability to sense a monofilament in his feet is well preserved. His Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=7.2\% $ (0.072)

Glomerular filtration rate = 78.6 mL/min per 1.73 m²

TSH = 1.40 mIU/L

You review with the patient appropriate strategies to reduce the risk of exercise-induced hypoglycemia, including use of temporarily lower basal rates or suspension of insulin infusion via the pump.

Which of the following is the best therapeutic intervention for this patient's diabetic neuropathic pain?
options: Adjust pump settings to intensify glycemic control
Add duloxetine to his pregabalin therapy
Add amitriptyline to his pregabalin therapy
Change from pregabalin to duloxetine therapy
Change from pregabalin to gabapentin therapy
items.explanation:
## 34 ANSWER: D) Change from pregabalin to duloxetine therapy

34 A. Approximately one-half of persons with diabetes develop peripheral neuropathy at some point in the course of their disease. The most common clinical manifestations are symptoms such as numbness and pain, typically symmetric and developing in the feet but later advancing proximally. Weakness due to motor involvement may occur but is less common than sensory deficits. Not all individuals with peripheral neuropathy experience neuropathic pain; however, when this does occur, it may adversely affect functional status and quality of life. Approximately 10% to 20% of persons with diabetes have painful peripheral neuropathy, although this may be an underestimate because patients do not consistently report their discomfort. Thus, many patients with clinical significant neuropathic pain are most likely untreated or inadequately treated.

Painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a result of damage to small, unmyelinated fibers rather than the large myelinated fibers responsible for proprioception and touch. Thus, as is the case for this patient, marked neuropathic damage may be present despite preservation of the latter senses. Normal responses to testing with monofilament, tuning fork, or even electrophysiologic testing do not exclude the possibility of painful diabetic neuropathy. Hyperglycemia contributes to the development of neuropathy in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, the pathophysiology that leads to this complication is most likely quite complex. In type 2 diabetes, concomitant conditions including hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance at the level of the neuron are probably contributory. More intensive glycemic control appears to significantly reduce the development of neuropathy in persons with type 1 diabetes, but it has not been consistently documented to be as successful in preventing this complication in type 2 diabetes. Importantly, improvement in hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ does not reduce neuropathic pain and, in fact, very rapid normalization of glycemia may in some instances worsen pain. Although intensification this patient's glycemic control (Answer A) may be otherwise indicated, this intervention would not be expected to improve his neuropathic symptoms.

Recent evidence-based guidelines for the treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy have been outlined by groups including the American Academies of Neurology, the American Association of Neuromuscular and Electrodiagnostic Medicine, and the American Association of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation. This set of guidelines and subsequently published review articles describe several first-line classes of drugs as effective treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy. These include voltage-gated calcium channel  $ \alpha_{2}\delta $ ligand anticonvulsant (pregabalin and gabapentin), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (venlafaxine and duloxetine), and tricyclic antidepressants. Of these agents, pregabalin and duloxetine are approved by the US Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Any of these drug classes is appropriate to use as initial therapy for the management of this condition. If on the maximally effective and/or tolerated dosage of one such medication there is no improvement in neuropathic pain, drug therapy should be changed in a first-line agent of a different drug class. This patient has not experienced any noticeable relief of pain with the maximal appropriate dosage of pregabalin; thus, the addition of duloxetine (Answer B) or amitriptyline (Answer C)

C) would not be the best next steps in management. However, if the patient had experienced partial relief of symptoms (described by some authors as a 30% or greater reduction in pain), then the addition of either of these agents to pregabalin therapy would be indicated. items.question:
A 19-year-old man is transitioning from the pediatric endocrinology clinic. In infancy, he was admitted to the hospital for new-onset hyperglycemia. He had presented with acute hyperglycemia (blood glucose concentration of 578 mg/dL [32.1 mmol/L]) associated with weight loss, polyuria, and polydipsia. He was initially treated with insulin and his regimen was subsequently transitioned to oral sulfonylurea treatment. He had done well with this treatment, reports good glycemic control, and has no diabetes-related complications. The patient's father has a history of diabetes mellitus that is well controlled with oral hypoglycemic therapy.

Physical examination findings are normal. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ measurement is 6.3% (0.063).

Which of the following tests should you order to establish the etiology of his hyperglycemia?
options: Islet-cell antibody assessment
Glutamic acid decarboxylase antibody assessment
Genetic testing for a GCK mutation (glucokinase gene)
Genetic testing for a FOXP3 mutation (forkhead box P3 gene)
Genetic testing for a KCNJ11 mutation (ATP-sensitive potassium channel gene)
items.explanation:
38 ANSWER: E) Genetic testing for a KCNJ11 mutation (ATP-sensitive potassium channel gene)

This patient initially presented with acute hyperglycemia consistent with the diagnosis of neonatal diabetes is categorized as transient or permanent. It is defined as permanent hyperglycemia if it occurs in the first few months of life, lasts more than 2 weeks, and requires medication for management. This patient has permanent neonatal diabetes on the basis of his history. The course of this condition and duration of hyperglycemia are dictated by the underlying genetic defect.

Mutations in the gene encoding the ATP-sensitive potassium channel (KCNJ11) (Answer E) can cause permanent neonatal diabetes mellitus, whereas mutations in the gene encoding the sulfonylurea receptor subunit (SUR1) (ABCC8) can result in either permanent or transient neonatal disease. The mutations that activate the ATP-sensitive potassium channel cause hyperpolarization of the  $ \beta $ cells, thereby preventing the release of insulin. The potassium ATP channel is composed of a small subunit (Kir6.2) that surrounds a central pore and 4 regulatory proteins with a KCNJ11 mutation may be small-for-gestational-age and have a constellation of SORBS1 subunits. Patients with a KCNJ11 mutation may be small-for-gestational-age and have a constellation of SORBS1 subunits. Patients with a KCNJ11 mutation may be small-for-gestational-age and have a constellation of SORBS1 subunits. Patients with a KCNJ11 mutation may

Most infants with transient neonatal diabetes have either paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 6 or an unbalanced duplication of paternal chromosome 6. Another cause of transient neonatal diabetes is a mutation in the imprinted gene ZAC/PLAGL1, a transcriptional regulator of the type 1 receptor for pituitary adenylate cyclase-activating polypeptide, which is an important regulator of insulin secretion. PLAGL1 is located on chromosome 6q24. Neonatal diabetes mellitus is also associated with mutations in other genes including RFX6, PDX1 (previously IPF1), EIF2AK3, FOXP3, PTF1A, GLIS3, and INS. Mutations in some of these genes result in pancreatic agenesis or hypoplasia or absent β cells. FOXP3 mutations (Answer D) cause a rare X-linked disorder that can result in a constellation of symptoms of IPEX syndrome (immune dysregulation, polyendocrinopathy, enteropathy). This patient does not have any of these features.

Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY) is the most common form of monogenic diabetes, and it is diagnosed in approximately 2% to 5% of patients with diabetes. This condition is usually diagnosed in young adults (<25 years) and is associated with an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Thus, in affected families, more than 2 generations often have a history of diabetes. Persons with MODY 2 have a defect in the glucokinase enzyme (encoded by the GCK gene [Answer C]), which phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate and acts as a glucose sensor. Affected persons experience a higher threshold for glucose-stimulated insulin secretion. The hyperglycemia resulting from this defect is a mild, stable fasting hyperglycemia, and affected persons are not able to develop diabetes-associated complications. Thus, they can simply be treated with lifestyle modification. This patient had severe hyperglycemia as a neonate, which responded well to sulfonylurea treatment, and he is very unlikely to have a mutation associated with monogenic diabetes.

He did not present with typical biochemical features of diabetic ketoacidosis and had a normal anion gap despite severe hyperglycemia, making the underlying diagnosis of type 1 diabetes very unlikely. Therefore, he is unlikely to have detectable islet-cell antibodies (Answer A) or glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies (Answer B).

##### Educational Objective:

Determine the underlying etiology of neonatal diabetes mellitus.

items.question:
A 38-year-old man with a 7-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus presents for help attaining better glycemic control. His diabetes is currently being managed with insulin glargine, 19 units at bedtime, and insulin glulisine, 7 units just before he starts a meal. He tries to keep the carbohydrate content of foods consistent at meal times, and he does not snack between meals or before bed. Supplemental insulin glulisine is also used to lower high glucose levels: 1 unit to lower his glucose 40 mg/dL (2.22 mmol/L) to a target of 120 mg/dL (6.66 mmol/L). His total daily insulin dose averages about 40 units. He has been adhering to recommended home glucose monitoring 4 times daily—before meals and at bedtime. Mild symptoms of hypoglycemia occur 3 times per week on average, and they can occur at any time of day and occasionally overnight. His downloaded glucose meter for the past 3 months reveals a mean glucose concentration of 131 mg/dL (7.27 mmol/L) with a range of 47 to 410 mg/dL (2.61 to 22.76 mmol/L) and a standard deviation of 47 mg/dL (2.61 mmol/L). Glucose levels are mildly elevated and mildly variable throughout the day, with no clear trends in high or low glucose readings, and they are mostly stable overnight.

He is up to date on screening examinations and laboratory studies, and he has no evidence of diabetes complications. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level is 7.5% (0.075) today. A laboratory-measured fasting glucose concentration is 113 mg/dL (6.27 mmol/L) (simultaneous meter glucose reading is 115 mg/dL [6.38 mmol/L]). He is frustrated because he has been following insulin-dosing instructions; he would like to lower his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ to help prevent long-term diabetes complications.

Which of the following strategies would be expected to safely lower his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level?
options: Increase the insulin glargine dose by 1 to 2 units daily
Change his bolus insulin glulisine from set doses to an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of 1:8
Change his correction factor to 1 unit to lower his glucose 30 mg/dL (1.66 mmol/L)
Move his insulin glulisine dose to 15 minutes before meals
Instruct him to eat more high-glycemic index foods
items.explanation:
## 42 ANSWER: D) Move his insulin glulisine dose to 15 minutes before meals

42 $ ^{A} $ This patient's mean glucose concentration of 131 mg/dL (7.27 mmol/L) using measurements tested before meals and at bedtime is lower than one would expect on the basis of his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level. A hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ reading of 7.5% (0.075) equates to a mean blood glucose concentration of about 165 mg/dL (9.16 mmol/L). This supports the idea that hyperglycemia is occurring at times when glucose levels are not tested, namely in the postprandial state. Continuous glucose monitoring has demonstrated that unrecognized postprandial hyperglycemia is common in patients with type 1 diabetes and that it contributes adversely to overall glycemic control as measured by hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $.

Postprandial hyperglycemia can be confirmed by having patients test fingerstick glucose levels after meals. Peak glucose levels occur 1 to 2 hours after meals, depending on meal composition. Foods with a higher glycemic index (low in fiber and/or higher in simple sugars) lead to a more rapid and higher peak glucose level than do foods with a lower glycemic index (thus, Answer E is incorrect). The maximum insulin level after injection of one of the rapid-acting insulin analogues occurs about 90 minutes after injection. Reduced postprandial glucose excursions are seen if the insulin injection is moved from immediately before the meal to 15 minutes before the meal (thus, Answer D is correct).

The insulin sensitivity factor can be calculated using the following equation: 1800/total daily insulin dose. In this case, with the patient using about 40 units of insulin daily, the calculated sensitivity factor would be 1:45, or 1 unit to drop the glucose concentration 45 mg/dL (2.50 mmol/L). In this vignette, increasing the patient's sensitivity factor to 1:30 (Answer C) could lead to increased hypoglycemia.

Two-hour postmeal glucose levels are a better predictor of cardiovascular events and mortality than fasting glucose levels, and they are a better predictor of retinopathy in type 2 diabetes mellitus than hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $. Postprandial hyperglycemia increases oxidative stress and is associated with endothelial dysfunction in patients with type 2 diabetes. Compared with regular insulin, rapid-acting insulin analogues lead to better postprandial glycemic control in patients with type 1 diabetes and has been shown to improve cardiovascular risk markers. However, randomized prospective trials targeting postprandial hyperglycemia have not shown reductions in cardiovascular outcomes in patients with type 2 diabetes and have not been performed in patients with type 1 diabetes.

Interventions that lower postprandial glucose responses to a meal include use of rapid-acting insulin analogues rather than human regular insulin, the consumption of lower-glycemic index carbohydrates, warming the injection site before an insulin bolus, the use of continuous glucose monitors, and bolusing insulin 15 minutes before meals rather than immediately before. The addition of protein to a meal increases the postmeal glucose response and the late postmeal insulin requirement. Improvements in postprandial glucose levels can be documented by having a patient check postmeal glucose levels before and after making the change in timing of the premeal rapid-acting insulin dose.

Prandial insulin coverage can effectively control postprandial glucose levels either with set insulin doses within fixed carbohydrate amounts at mealtimes or by having patients learn to count the grams of carbohydrates in a meal and adjust the prandial insulin dose based on a calculated insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio. A formula commonly used to calculate an individual patient's insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio is 500/total daily insulin dose. 42 $ ^{A} $ This patient's mean glucose concentration of 131 mg/dL (7.27 mmol/L) using measurements tested before meals and at bedtime is lower than one would expect on the basis of his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level. A hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ reading of 7.5% (0.075) equates to a mean blood glucose concentration of about 165 mg/dL (9.16 mmol/L). This supports the idea that hyperglycemia is occurring at times when glucose levels are not tested, namely in the postprandial state. Continuous glucose monitoring has demonstrated that unrecognized postprandial hyperglycemia is common in patients with type 1 diabetes and that it contributes adversely to overall glycemic control as measured by hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $.

Postprandial hyperglycemia can be confirmed by having patients test fingerstick glucose levels after meals. Peak glucose levels occur 1 to 2 hours after meals, depending on meal composition. Foods with a higher glycemic index (low in fiber and/or higher in simple sugars) lead to a more rapid and higher peak glucose level than do foods with a lower glycemic index (thus, Answer E is incorrect). The maximum insulin level after injection of one of the rapid-acting insulin analogues occurs about 90 minutes after injection. Reduced postprandial glucose excursions are seen if the insulin injection is moved from immediately before the meal to 15 minutes before the meal (thus, Answer D is correct).

The insulin sensitivity factor can be calculated using the following equation: 1800/total daily insulin dose. In this case, with the patient using about 40 units of insulin daily, the calculated sensitivity factor would be 1:45, or 1 unit to drop the glucose concentration 45 mg/dL (2.50 mmol/L). A 65-year-old man with a history of dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease, and uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus has been referred to you for glycemic management. Despite continued up-titration of his insulin dosing, his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level has consistently remained markedly elevated. For the past 6 months, his medication regimen has included maximally dosed metformin; insulin glargine, 75 units injected subcutaneously twice daily; and regular insulin, 100 units injected subcutaneously 3 times daily before meals. The patient reports good adherence to his prescribed medication regimen, with no problems understanding or accurately administering his insulin doses. He does share that he has trouble with dietary indiscretion and minimal regular exercise despite having seen a nutritionist and diabetes nurse educator for lifestyle counseling on several previous occasions. He checks his blood glucose levels several times daily. His blood glucose log reveals blood glucose concentrations that generally range between 200 and 250 mg/dL (11.10-13.88 mmol/L) before meals and often close to 300 mg/dL (16.65 mmol/L) at bedtime. He has never had problems with hypoglycemia.

Blood pressure is 140/81 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 85 beats/min. Height is 66 in (167.6 cm), and weight is 223 lb (101.4 kg) (BMI = 36 kg/m²). He has no palpable lipohypertrophy or other abnormalities identified at his insulin injection sites. Skin changes consistent with acanthosis nigricans are present.

Recent laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=9.1\% $ (0.091)

Glomerular filtration rate = 71.4 mL/min per 1.73 m²

Creatinine = 1.1 mg/dL (97.2 μmol/L)

TSH = 2.40 mIU/L

Which of the following is the best next step in this patient's care?
options: Change to U500 regular insulin, 0.20 mL (100 units) 3 times daily before meals
Change to U500 regular insulin, 0.45 mL (225 units) twice daily before meals
Change to U500 regular insulin, 0.30 mL (150 units) 3 times daily before meals
Change to U500 regular insulin,0.40 mL (200 units)3 times daily before meals
Refer for insulin pump training in preparation for initiating U500 regular insulin via continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion
items.explanation:
48 ANSWER: C) Change to U500 regular insulin, 0.30 mL (150 units) 3 times daily before meals

This patient has very poor glycemic control despite use of 450 units of U100 insulin daily. He has severe insulin resistance, generally defined as an insulin requirement greater than 200 units of insulin daily. Use of such very high insulin doses is inconvenient because multiple injections per dose are required and high volumes may lead to injection site discomfort. In addition, the absorption of insulin from large-volume subcutaneous depots may be poor and/or unpredictable. In patients such as this, conversion from U100 to 5-fold concentrated U500 regular insulin (U500R) represents a very reasonable alternative glycemic management strategy. In particular, conversion to U500R permits administration of large insulin doses via much smaller volumes per injection and fewer injections per day, which in turn may improve insulin absorption and perhaps regimen adherence. Studies of U500R insulin action suggest that this insulin formulation has an onset of action similar to that of U100 regular insulin and a duration of action similar to that of U100 NPH insulin. Small studies of patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes who switch from U100 to U500R insulin have demonstrated improvements in hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level ranging from 1.0% to 3.29%, without a significant increase in rates of severe hypoglycemia. In addition, conversion to U500R in such patients has been associated with improvements in patient satisfaction with treatment and quality of life. Although U500R insulin vials are more expensive than U100 insulin vials, this does not necessarily increase the overall costs of care. The per-unit cost of U500R is estimated to be less than that of U100 insulin, and the costs of needles and syringes may also be reduced because fewer injections are needed.

Although no significant increase in severe hypoglycemia has been reported in clinical trials, use of U500R in clinical practice has resulted in instances of clinically significant hypoglycemia because of dosing errors. Presently, U500R is available only in a vial, and thus must be drawn up and administered via a syringe. Some prescribers prefer to have patients draw up U500R in a U100 insulin syringe with guidance to adapt these devices for the more concentrated insulin. However, the Institute for Safe Medication Practices has recommended “consistent use of a

tuberculin syringe with U-500 insulin, with total doses expressed in terms of both units and volume, i.e., 200 units (0.4 mL). This recommendation has been reiterated in a recent publication dated October 2013. Irrespective of type of syringe chosen for insulin administration, the patient and any associated caregivers must be fully educated regarding the correct drawing up and administration of prescribed doses. Very careful attention must be paid to insulin dosing when users of U500R are hospitalized, as many reported errors in insulin dosing have occurred in hospitalized patients. Guidelines to enhance the safety of insulin use during hospitalizations have also been issued by the Institute for Safe Medication Practices. Delivery devices to administer concentrated insulin more safely currently under development.

Recommendations for the conversion from U100 insulin to U500R generally incorporate the baseline U100 insulin doses, as well as hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level, to determine appropriate doses of the concentrated insulin. In general, experts recommend that the total daily insulin dose be increased by 10% in patients with a hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level above 10% (>0.100); that the total daily insulin dose be maintained in patients with a hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level between 8% and 10% (0.080 and 0.100); and that the total daily dose be decreased by 10% to 20% in patients with a hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level below 8% (<0.080) at the time of conversion. items.question:
A 59-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 11 years ago), hypercholesterolemia, and hypertension presents for evaluation of acute-onset pain followed by weakness in her left leg and hip. The pain has been present for 3 weeks and the weakness started 5 days ago. She has not identified exacerbating or relieving factors for her symptoms and did not experience any preceding trauma. The discomfort is aching in quality and only present on the left side. Because of the weakness, she is having difficulty lifting her left leg to get into her car or into bed. Her bowel and bladder function has been normal.

She takes metformin and glyburide for type 2 diabetes mellitus, hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension, and atorvastatin for hypercholesterolemia.

On physical examination, she appears uncomfortable. Weight is 130 lb (59.1 kg), and height is 63 in (160 cm) (BMI = 23 kg/m²). Her weight has decreased by 6 lb (2.7 kg) in the last 3 months. Blood pressure is 100/75 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 86 beats/min. Her thyroid gland is normal without dominant nodularity. She has mild loss of distal sensation on Semmes-Weinstein monofilament testing. Only the left quadriceps reflex is present; other reflexes are absent. Vibration and position sense are normal. You are able to elicit left hip and thigh pain with

movement. There is no pain on palpation of the muscles. There is no tremor of the outstretched hands. Distal pulses are intact and easily palpable.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=7.5\% $ (0.075)

Urinary albumin excretion = 10 mg/24 h

Sodium = 138 mEq/L (138 mmol/L)

Creatine phosphokinase = 60 U/L (1.0 μkat/L)

 $$  Potassium=4.5mEq/L(4.5mmol/L) $$ 

Complete blood cell count, normal (specifically, normal hematocrit and MCV)

 $$  Creatinine=0.8\ mg/dL(70.7\ \mu mol/L) $$ 

TSH = 0.5 mIU/L

You arrange for needle electromyography, which documents fibrillation potentials; decreased motor unit recruitment; and long-duration, high-amplitude motor unit action potentials.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Diabetic amyotrophy
Vitamin B_{12} deficiency
Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy
Meralgia paresthetica
Muscle infarct
items.explanation:
## 51 ANSWER: A) Diabetic amyotrophy

Diabetic amyotrophy (Answer A) is also known as Bruns-Garland syndrome. Because of the considerable diabetic neuropathy, diabetic polyradiculopathy, diabetic motor neuropathy, diabetic radiculoplexopathy, diabetic lumbosacral plexopathy, and diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy. However, diabetic amyotrophy

affects both the lumbosacral nerve roots and peripheral nerves; thus, it is not a pure lumbosacral plexopathy as is the case with idiopathic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy. Although lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy is high on the differential diagnosis list, it is not associated with diabetes. This, along with the lack of involvement of peripheral nerves, helps distinguish it from diabetic neuropathy.

Diabetic amyotrophy is seen at any stage of diabetes neuropathy. in middle-aged patients with reasonable diabetes control or in those with a recent diabetes diagnosis. Sometimes diabetic amyotrophy can be the presenting feature of diabetes. It is more prevalent in patients with type 2 diabetes or type 1 diabetes. Concomitant weight loss is common. Affected persons usually have unilateral proximal hypoflexia due to the loss of the body'sி nteroverset with the lack of involvement of the type of diabetes in the body.

dyspnea is often found as a subset of patients have no associated pain; weakens; weight loss; and autonomic dysfunction such as orthostatic dysfunction, tachycardia, altered sweating, etc. Upper limb or thoracic involvement may occur. Affected patients may present with asymmetric weakness involving the proximal extremity. However, over time (months), the signs and symptoms progress to the contralateral extremity and become symmetric. Neurophysiologic testing typically shows a predominant axonal neuropathy, and nerve biopsy may document microvasculitis. Neuroimaging can be used to exclude other differential diagnoses. Nerve conduction studies reveal marked reduction in amplitudes of the compound muscle and sensory nerve action potentials, while conduction velocities are not as significant. The diagnosis of diabetic amyotrophy is made predominantly on the basis of a conglomeration of

Treatment includes narcotic analgesic agents, antidepressant medications, and occupational and physical therapy. Immune-modulating therapy or steroid therapy is not helpful. Complete recovery is uncommon, but clinical improvement can be accomplished with antidepressants, physical therapy, patient education, and reassurance.

Vitamin B $ _{12} $ deficiency (Answer B) usually presents with symmetric neuropathy that affects the legs more than the arms. The initial presenting symptom is paresthesias and ataxia associated with loss of vibration and position sense, which may progress to severe weakness, spasticity, clonus, paraplegia, and even fecal and urinary incontinence.

Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (Answer C) generally presents with symmetric weakness with various sensory symptoms that exhibit either a relapsing, stepwise progressive, or steadily progressive course. In addition to limb weakness, affected patients may also present with fatigue, numbness, tingling, or tight sensation in their extremities. Pain is rarely a component of chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Diminished or absent reflexes on neurologic testing is the rule. Cranial nerve involvement occurs in some cases and loss of micturition control due to involvement of pelvic neural circuitry is relatively common. Meralgia paresthetica (Answer D) is characterized by burning pain, tingling, numbness, and decreased sensation over the lateral thigh, corresponding to the distribution of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. This nerve does not contain motor fibers, so affected patients do not have motor deficits such as those observed in the vignette.

A 62-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by nonproliferative retinopathy and microalbuminuria returns to see you for a follow-up appointment and management of his blood glucose levels. His diabetes was initially treated with oral antihyperglycemic medications; however, he required the addition of insulin to his regimen approximately 5 years ago. In addition to metformin, he takes 2 daily injections of 75% insulin lispro protamine suspension/25% insulin lispro injection (75/25 insulin) with his morning and evening meals. Because of persistently elevated hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ levels, his insulin daily doses have been repeated increased up to his current regimen of 50 units before breakfast and 30 units before his evening meal. The patient reports consistently eating 3 meals per day, generally at 7:00 AM, 11:30 AM, and 7:00 PM. He had no difficulty remembering to take his insulin doses as prescribed, and he reports good adherence to his medical therapy. He has expressed frustration over his hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ values, as his fingerstick glucose levels checked twice daily before administration of his insulin doses are almost always 150 mg/dL (8.32 mmol/L) or lower. He had no problems with hypoglycemia or hypoglycemia unawareness. On physical examination, blood pressure is 138/75 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 80 beats/min. Height is 71 in (180.3 cm), and weight is 196 lb (89.1 kg) (BMI = 27.3 kg/m $ ^{2} $). His point-of-care hemoglobin $ A_{tc} $ level at today's visit is unchanged at 9.1% (0.091). The patient has worn a continuous glucose monitor for 5 days before today's office visit. He reports no notal alteration in his medication use, dietary habits, or physical activity during this period. The data obtained from the continuous glucose monitor are displayed below.
Continuous Glucose Sensor Data, mg/dL
Which of the following is the best next step in this patient's care?
options: Give the patient a new blood glucose testing meter
Change timing of blood glucose testing to 2 hours after the start of each meal
Change to 45 units of once-daily basal, long-acting insulin and 15 units of rapid-acting insulin before meals
Modify 75/25 insulin dosing to 60 units before breakfast and 30 units before the evening meal
Modify 75/25 insulin dosing to 50 units before breakfast and 40 units before the evening meal
items.explanation:
## 55 ANSWER: C) Change to 45 units of once-daily basal, long-acting insulin and 15 units of rapid-acting insulin before meals This patient with type 2 diabetes mellitus has what appears to be a marked discrepancy between his home capillary plasma glucose readings and his hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ values. Although not specifically addressed in this question, this discordance is sometimes due to conditions such as anemia, hemoglobinopathy, or clinically significant renal dysfunction, which may contribute to unreliable hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ results. In the absence of such a condition, self-monitored blood glucose values much lower than that suggested by the hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ level should prompt an investigation for hyperglycemia occurring at times other than those routinely monitored. This patient has undergone continuous glucose monitoring over a consecutive 5-day period. As displayed in the accompanying image, the data obtained during this monitoring reveal consistent and significant increases in his glucose levels following the midday and evening meals.
Continuous Glucose Sensor Data, mg/dL
Interestingly, the patient's glucose values before breakfast and the evening meal are generally in the much lower range that he had reported. Giving the patient a new blood glucose testing meter (Answer A) does not appear necessary because the glucose sensor data coincide nicely with his reported values. Changing the timing of his blood glucose testing to 2 hours after the start of each meal (Answer B) may well have been the simplest way to detect his postprandial hyperglycemia. However, this is not the best next step in his care because adequate data are available now to modify the patient's insulin regimen. In general, the goal for the peak postprandial capillary glucose concentration is less than 180 mg/dL (<9.99 mmol/L) checked 1 to 2 hours after the start of meals. This patient's postprandial glucose values often exceed this goal considerably. American Diabetes Association guidelines suggest that it is appropriate to target postprandial hyperglycemia if the hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ level remains elevated despite adequate preprandial control.
Modification of this patient's insulin regimen to 1 injection daily of basal, long-acting insulin and rapid-acting insulin doses given at all meals (Answer C) is the step that will most effectively address postprandial control of glucose at all times of day. The insulin doses outlined in this answer (45 units of once-daily basal, long-acting insulin and 15 units of rapid-acting insulin before meals) provide 50% of his total daily insulin dose as basal, and divide the remaining 50% among his 3 meals. Continued and more frequent blood glucose testing is also required to appropriately adjust his insulin doses from this initial regimen, particularly if some also are much larger and/or higher in carbohydrates than others. Modification of the patient's 75/25 insulin dosing to 60 units before breakfast and 30 units before the evening (Answer D) may have unpredictable results. Although the increased morning dose might to some extent be the rise in glucose following the midday meal, it is unclear whether the peak effect of the morning dose is fixed insulin will coincide closely with the absorption of carbohydrates from that meal. In addition, given the minimal rise in blood glucose after breakfast, it is possible that an increased dose of mixed insulin in the ongoing may result in hypoglycemia before the midday meal. Furthermore, this dosing change does not address hyperglycemia that occurs after the evening meal. ## 59 ANSWER: D) Review the angiogram with the radiologist

59 ANSWER:

The results of the selective arterial calcium stimulation test cannot be interpreted without knowledge of the arterial anatomy of the pancreas. An angiogram is performed at the start of the procedure to guide catheter placement and is important to the interpretation of the results. In this vignette, the results would, at face value, suggest either the presence of multiple islet tumors or more rare conditions such as noninsulinoma, pancreatogenous hypoglycemia syndrome with diffuse involvement of the pancreas. In approximately 75% of the normal population, the gastroduodenal artery supplies the head of the pancreas, the superior mesenteric artery supplies part of the body, and the splenic artery supplies the remainder of the pancreas, including the tail. This information is usually helpful in regionalizing (but not, strictly speaking, localizing) insulinomas. However, because of the inherent frequency of anomalies in the arterial supply of the pancreas, it is important that the test results be interpreted in light of the arterial anatomy of the individual patient.

In this vignette, the patient's arterial circulation had acquired an abnormality—namely, an atherosclerotic stenosis of the celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery with the development of an extensive collateral network so that injection of calcium into any 1 of the 3 main arterial trunks was accompanied by significant retrograde flow along the collateral circulation. Therefore, the selective arterial calcium stimulation test results are useless in regionalizing her tumor. Consulting with the radiologist (Answer D) is the best next step.

Before the advent of arterial stimulation testing or other invasive studies, intraoperative ultrasonography to localize insulinomas. Although it may be reasonable to recommend surgery (Answer B) on the basis of the available data, there are no data in the clinical vignette to support a distal pancreatectomy since an insulinoma could not be regionalized to the pancreatic tail and could be anywhere in the pancreas. Blind distal pancreatectomy is rarely indicated.

Compared with CT, MRI (Answer C) has inferior resolving power because of the motion artifact associated with breathing, and it is usually not used in the evaluation of insulinoma.

Positron emission tomography (Answer E) is helpful in managing hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia in infancy but it is far from certain whether this is superior to conventional imaging in the evaluation of hypoglycemic disorders in adults. Fluoro F 18 DOPA has been used in pediatric hypoglycemia, although there are case reports of radiolabeled glucagonlike peptide 1 receptor agonists being used for this purpose. Their role and superiority over conventional imaging are debatable.

A 29-year-old woman with polycystic ovary syndrome returns to your clinic for a routine follow-up appointment. She has a history of obesity, hirsutism, irregular menses, and infertility, but has no current plans for pregnancy. A previous evaluation for Cushing syndrome revealed no evidence of hypercortisolism. Prediabetes was diagnosed approximately 2 years ago on the basis of an elevated fasting glucose concentration and a hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ value of 5.7% (0.057). At that time, the patient received comprehensive lifestyle education with an emphasis on weight loss through caloric restriction and regular physical activity. She reports that she exercises regularly and that she has been able to lose approximately 5% of her initial body weight, primarily by reducing her fat intake. The patient has taken metformin at a dosage of 1000 mg twice daily for the past year; she has tolerated the medication well and has had no difficulty with adherence.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 135/78 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 88 beats/min. Height is 63 in (160 cm), and weight is 317 lb (144.1 kg) (BMI = 56.1 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Her weight is unchanged from that documented at her last appointment 4 months ago.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=6.1\% $ (0.061)

Creatinine = 0.7 mg/dL (61.9 μmol/L)

Sodium = 141 mEq/L (141 mmol/L)

Glomerular filtration rate = 104 mL/min per 1.73 m²

Potassium = 3.8 mEq/L (3.8 mmol/L)

Fasting glucose = 115 mg/dL (6.38 mmol/L)

Which of the following is the best management plan?
options: Substitute an $ \alpha $-glucosidase inhibitor for metformin
Substitute a thiazolidinedione for metformin
Begin an ACE inhibitor
Recommend changing to a very low-carbohydrate diet
Refer to a bariatric surgeon for consultation
