A 59-year-old woman presents for assistance with diabetes control. She was first noted to have diabetes-range fasting hyperglycemia on screening laboratory tests at age 19 years. This followed a 15-lb (6.8 kg) weight gain during her freshman year at college. She was placed on a low-concentrated sweets diet, and she successfully lost weight and was not followed up closely thereafter. At age 29 years, hyperglycemia was again not on routine blood work. Her BMI was 20.8 kg/m². Referral to an endocrinologist led to a presumptive diagnosis of type 1 diabetes, and she was prescribed a multiple daily injection insulin regimen.

Over the past 10 years, her hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ levels have ranged from 6.3% to 7.2% (0.063 to 0.072). There are no known diabetes complications. She eats a low-carbohydrate, low-fat diet and exercises for 90 minutes daily. Stakes bupropion for depression and alendronate for osteoporosis.

Her current insulin regimen consists of insulin glargine, 13 units at bedtime, and insulin aspart, 4 to 6 units before meals. She has been measuring glucose levels 0 to 2 times daily since she started insulin therapy, mostly fasting and at bedtime. Her 3-month glucose average is 132 mg/dL (7.33 mmol/L) with a range of 28 to 250 mg/d (1.55 to 13.88 mmol/L). Hypoglycemia occurs mostly overnight. She has a history of hypoglycemia unawareness and has had 3 instances of severe hypoglycemia since her diagnosis.

Her family history is notable for insulin-requiring diabetes mellitus diagnosed in her father at age 20 years and diabetes mellitus diagnosed in her paternal grandmother in her 20s.

On physical examination, height is 65 in (165.1 cm) and weight is 118 lb (53.6 kg) (BMI = 19.6 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Her blood pressure is 117/55 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 66 beats/min. She has normal findings on funduscopic examination, normal peripheral pulses, normal findings on peripheral nerve examination, and no acanthosis nigricans.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} = 7.0\% $ (0.070)

Creatinine = 0.78 mg/dL (70.0 μmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 135 mg/dL (3.49 mmol/L)

Urinary albumin = 6.8 mg/g creatinine

Triglycerides = 82 mg/dL (0.93 mmol/L)

C-peptide (fasting) = 0.63 ng/mL (0.21 nmol/L)

Total cholesterol = 276 mg/dL (7.15 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 125 mg/dL (3.24 mmol/L)

Glucose (fasting) = 154 mg/dL (8.55 mmol/L)

#### Which of the following actions would most significantly affect her diabetes management?
options: Instructing her in carbohydrate counting to reduce glucose vanity.
Increasing the frequency of glucose monitoring to 4 times daily and when she has any symptoms of hypoglycemia.
Reducing her insulin aspart dose at the meal before she exercises.
Referring her for genetic testing.
Switching to insulin pump therapy.
items.explanation:
## 68 ANSWER: D) Referring her for genetic testing This patient's presentation is at

This patient's presentation is atypical, and it warrants a reassessment of her diabetes diagnosis. Type 1 diabetes was diagnosed on the basis of hyperglycemia in a young adult with a lean body weight and healthful lifestyle. Adult-onset type 1 diabetes often progresses more slowly, with residual endogenous insulin production persisting for some time after the diagnosis. However, it would be atypical if type 1 diabetes did not progress more symptomatic hyperglycemia and even ketoacidosis if treated with lifestyle measures alone for 10 years.

Also atypical for type 1 diabetes is the easily measureable C-peptide level, the absence of any apparent diabetes complications 40 years after the initial diagnosis, and overall good glycemic control in the setting of infrequent glucose monitoring.

Her physical examination findings, lack of dyslipidemia, and low insulin requirements argue against insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes. The family history of 2 paternal generations of diabetes diagnosed at a young account for about 2% of all diabetes cases, and it is often misclassified as type 1 or type 2 diabetes. Mutations in different genes have been described, all involved in the development, function, or regulation of pancreatic cells, leading to abnormal insulin secretion. Most cases of MODY are inherited in an autosomal dominant man although spontaneous de novo mutations can occur. MODY 3 causes up to 60% of MODY cases, and it is caused by a mutation in the HNF1A gene, which encodes a β-cell transcription factor. Affected patients are very sensitive to sulfonylureas, as are patients with MODY 1 who account for up to 10% of MODY cases. About 30% to 40% of patients with MODY 3 or MODY 1 eventually require insulin to control their diabetes. Persons with MODY 2 who have a mutation in the glucokinase gene (GCK) account for 25% to 30% of MODY cases. MODY 2 is nonprogressive and can typically be treated with diet alone.

The patient in this vignette did not have type 1 diabetes-related antibodies. She eventually had genetic testing which confirmed the diagnosis of MODY 3. Her HDL-cholesterol level is high, as is commonly seen in patients with MODY 3. Her insulin aspart was discontinued, her insulin glargine dosage was reduced, and glimepiride was added to her regimen. She was eventually weaned off insulin glargine completely. Her hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level was 6.3% (0.063) on glimepiride, 2 mg daily. She continued to maintain a healthful lifestyle, and she experienced mild fasting hyperglycemia and no further hypoglycemia.

Genetic testing for MODY (Answer D) should be considered in patients with a type 1 diabetes diagnosis who still produce insulin years after their initial diagnosis, in younger patients in whom type 2 diabetes is diagnosed who are not significantly overweight and do not have signs of insulin resistance, and in those with an autosomal dominant pattern of diabetes in the family pedigree. Because genetic testing remains quite expensive, a sulfonylurea trial in a patient suspected to have MODY can also be undertaken, and if it were successful, it would similarly provide an effective treatment regimen. Instruction in carbohydrate counting to reduce glucose variability (Answer A), increasing the frequency of glucose monitoring to 4 times daily and when she has any symptoms of hypoglycemia (Answer B), reducing her insulin aspart dose at the meal before she exercises (Answer C), or switching to insulin pump therapy (Answer E) can all potentially improve glycemic control in patients with type 1 diabetes, but they would have less of an impact on this patient's management than confirming a MODY diagnosis.

##### Educational Objective:

Reassess the diagnosis of type 1 diabetes mellitus in a patient with an atypical presentation and review the various forms of maturity-onset diabetes of the young.

items.question:
A 26-year-old man presents to your office accompanied by his wife after he experienced a severe hypoglycemic reaction. Type 1 diabetes mellitus was diagnosed 15 years ago, and he has no known diabetes diagnosis. He has enjoyed the convenience of twice-daily insulin dosing with NPH insulin and insulin lispro

before breakfast and dinner. His current insulin regimen consists of NPH insulin, 20 units, and insulin lispro, 6 units, before breakfast, and NPH insulin, 8 units, and insulin lispro, 8 units, before dinner.

He has been measuring his glucose levels at home 0 to 2 times daily when he feels glucose levels are running high or low. Low glucose levels leading to slowed mentation and fatigue occur several times a week, and they range from 35 to 50 mg/dL (1.94 to 2.78 mmol/L) when tested with symptoms.

He refereed 2 football games on a Saturday afternoon and evening. The next morning, shortly after eating a large breakfast and taking his toddler on a long stroller walk, his wife found him seizing in the child's room. His fingerstick glucose concentration was 26 mg/dL (1.44 mmol/L), and his wife immediately administered glucagon. After a long period of confusion, he refused transfer to an emergency department by an ambulance crew. He recalls only feeling tired before losing consciousness.

On physical examination, findings from his cardiac and peripheral nerve examinations are normal. There are no significant orthostatic changes in blood pressure or pulse rate. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level in your office is 6.7% (0.067), and his 11:30-AM fingerstick glucose measurement is 68 mg/dL (3.77 mmol/L).

### In addition to increasing the frequency of home glucose monitoring and placing him on a more physiologic insulin regimen, which of the following is the most important recommendation to make now?
options: Reduce his physical activity
Increase his intake of high glycemic index foods
Have him pay greater attention to symptoms of hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia to guide premeal insulin-likeடோ dose adjustments
Change from set insulin lispro doses to an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of 1 unit to 6 g of carbohydrate
Raise glycemic targets in the short-term
items.explanation:
## 76 ANSWER: E) Raise glycemic targets in the short-term

76 Hypoglycemic unawareness is a common problem in patients with tightly controlled type 1 diabetes. Loss of adrenergic symptoms of hypoglycemia is more frequent with increasing duration of diabetes, and it is a major risk factor for severe hypoglycemia requiring assistance. Recurrent hypoglycemia reduces the normal counterregulatory hormone response of glucagon and epinephrine secretion to low glucose levels and decreases hypoglycemia awareness. Antecedent hypoglycemia was identified in the Diabetes Control and Complications Trial as the most common predictor of a future severe hypoglycemic event.

Although this patient's hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level has run consistently in a good range, his hypoglycemic unawareness indicates that good mean glucose levels are most likely occurring at the expense of recurrent hypoglycemia. Prevention of future severe hypoglycemia, a potentially life-threatening complication of insulin

therapy, should be improved by hypoglycemia for periods as short as 3 days has been shown to improve hypoglycemia awareness for up to 3 months and therefore reduce the risk of future hypoglycemia. Relaxing glycemic control in the short-term by raising glucose targets to prevent hypoglycemia (Answer E) will help reduce his risk of a recurrent severe hypoglycemic event.

increased frequency of blood glucose monitoring is critical to alert the patient to times of the day when glucose levels are running low and thus guide insulin dose reductions. This patient would be best advised to perform fingerstick glucose monitoring at minimum before meals and at bedtime, before driving, and when he has any symptoms of hypoglycemia (with or without use of a continuous glucose monitor). Studies have documented that patients are often inaccurate when estimating their glucose concentration unless levels are very high or low. Therefore, dosing insulin lispro on the basis of the perceived blood glucose level (Answer C) would not be expected to reduce his frequency of hypoglycemia.

Continuous glucose monitors should be considered for patients with persistent hypoglycemia unawareness because these devices can alert patients to unrecognized hypoglycemia. Continuous glucose monitors have been shown to lower hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ levels in adults and children with type 1 diabetes without increasing the rate of mild or severe hypoglycemia, and have been shown to reduce the frequency of severe hypoglycemia. A continuous glucose monitoring device has been introduced that communicates with an insulin pump and automatically suspends the basal insulin infusion when the glucose level is low and unrecognized. Use of this device reduces the amount of time spent in a hypoglycemic state overnight.

This patient's insulin regimen is nonphysiologic, and he would benefit from a change. Physiologic insulin replacement using a long-acting basal insulin and premeal rapid-acting insulin has been shown to lower the risk of hypoglycemia when compared with NPH-based insulin regimens. Insulin pump therapy, the most physiologic of insulin replacement regimens, would similarly be expected to reduce hypoglycemia rates in this patient. The intake of high glycemic index foods will acutely raise glucose levels, but such a dietary intervention (Answer B) is not an effective way prevent hypoglycemia in a patient on a nonphysiologic insulin regimen, and would lead to excessive postprandial hyperglycemia.

If this patient is found to have marked glucose variability because of variable carbohydrate intake at mealtimes, having him adjust his bolus insulin for anticipated carbohydrate intake using an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio should reduce glucose variability and rates of hypoglycemia. An insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio can be estimated from his total daily dose of insulin. A formula commonly used to calculate an individual patient's insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio is 500/total daily dose. His current total dai

She returns to your office to discuss the laboratory findings. Her blood pressure is again elevated (136/88 mm Hg, but her examination findings otherwise remain normal.

In the preconception counseling of this patient, which of the following should you recommend?
options: Delay conception until her glycemic control has improved as assessed by a hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ level less than 6.0% (<0.060)
Initiate treatment with an ACE inhibitor for renal protection and management of her blood pressure
Initiate treatment with methyldopa for management of her blood pressure

items.explanation:
78 ANSWER: D) Initiate treatment with methyldopa for management of her blood pressure

79 Women with pregestational type 1 diabetes may encounter several risks in pregnancy. Preconception counseling is very important to educate women about these risks and preempt exacerbation of existing complications or the development of new ones.

Uncontrolled hypertension can be a serious complication of pregnancy in women with diabetes and can precipitate retinal, renal, and cardiac dysfunction. ACE inhibitors (Answer C) are teratogenic, so they are intraindicated for this patient who is contemplating pregnancy despite the presence of microalbuminuria. Editions other than ACE inhibitors are the mainstay of hypertension treatment during pregnancy. Specifically, thyldopa (Answer D) has been used for many years, and it is safe for both the mother and developing fetus. Her antihypertensive agents that can safely be used during pregnancy include calcium channel blockers and drenergic blockers such as hydralazine. β-Adrenergic blockers can be used with caution.

Physiologic ocular changes that can occur in pregnancy include alterations of corneal sensitivity and thickness, reased tolerance to contact lenses, decreased intraocular pressure, hermalopia, and refractive errors. Pregnancy exacerbate preexisting retinopathy. Some women with diabetes develop de novo retinopathy during pregnancy, the development of any new ocular symptoms should be assessed promptly. Thus, dilated eye examinations

should ideally be performed before and during the first trimester of pregnancy, even in women such as this one who do not have preexisting retinopathy (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Subsequent retinal examinations should be based on the results of the preliminary examination. Retinal examinations should not be delayed until after completion of the pregnancy, as this strategy would miss de novo retinopathy if it occurs. In the Diabetes in Early Pregnancy Study that examined 140 women without proliferative retinopathy at conception, progression of retinopathy occurred in 20% of those with microaneurysms or mild nonproliferative retinopathy and in 55% of those with moderate-to-severe nonproliferative retinopathy. Ten percent of women

Hyperglycemia during the first trimester of pregnancy is associated with a higher rate of fetal loss. For this and other reasons, avoiding elevated glucose levels is a key preconception goal. However, low hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ values (such as <6.0% [<0.060]) could be associated with significant hypoglycemic events and is generally not ideal for most women with type 1 diabetes (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

Her TSH level is appropriate for pregnancy, and increasing her levothyroxine dosage (Answer E) is not warranted now. However, there will be an anticipated increase in her thyroid requirement once she is pregnant, and this should be managed appropriately.

Specifically, thyldopa (Answer D) has been used for many years, and it is safe for both the mother and developing fetus. Her antihypertensive agents that can safely be used during pregnancy include calcium channel blockers and drenergic blockers such as hydralazine. β-Adrenergic blockers can be used with caution.

Physiologic ocular changes that can occur in pregnancy include alterations of corneal sensitivity and thickness, reased tolerance to contact lenses, decreased intraocular pressure, hermalopia, and refractive errors. Pregnancy exacerbate preexisting retinopathy. Some women with diabetes develop de novo retinopathy during pregnancy, the development of any new ocular symptoms should be assessed promptly. Thus, dilated eye examinations

should ideally be performed before and during the first trimester of pregnancy, even in women such as this one who do not have preexisting retinopathy (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Subsequent retinal examinations should be based on the results of the preliminary examination. Retinal examinations should not be delayed until after completion of the pregnancy, as this strategy would miss de novo retinopathy if it occurs. In the Diabetes in Early Pregnancy Study that examined 140 women without proliferative retinopathy at conception, progression of retinopathy occurred in 20% of those with microaneurysms or mild nonproliferative retinopathy and in 55% of those with moderate-to-severe nonproliferative retinopathy. items.question:
A 49-year-old woman with no notable medical history is referred for evaluation of symptoms suspicious for hypoglycemia. These symptoms are most likely to occur when she has had no caloric intake for a period exceeding 4 to 5 hours, and the episodes are characterized by sweating, tremor, tachycardia, and confusion. Exercise worsens the frequency and severity of symptoms. She has had these symptoms for at least 6 months.

During a spontaneous episode of hypoglycemia, the following laboratory values are documented:

Glucose = 54 mg/dL (3.00 mmol/L)

C-peptide = 1.8 ng/mL (0.60 nmol/L)

β-Hydroxybutyrate = 0.1 mg/dL (9.6 μmol/L)

Insulin = 6.3 μIU/mL (43.8 pmol/L)

Insulin antibodies, undetectable

Proinsulin = 273 pg/mL (31 pmol/L)

Sulfonylurea screen (including repaglinide and glimepiride), negat

Triple-phase CT of the abdomen shows a 0.6-cm enhancing mass in the head of the pancreas consistent with an islet-cell tumor. The tumor is subsequently enucleated successfully and her symptoms resolve. Pathologic examination confirms that an insulinoma was resected.

Fourteen months later, the patient returns with symptoms similar to those she experienced before her operation. Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia is again documented. Neither abdominal CT nor endoscopic ultrasonography reveals a tumor.

### Which of the following is the most appropriate management step now?
options: No further testing needed; initiate medical therapy with diazoxide or a long-acting somatostatin analogue to prevent hypoglycemia
Order a selective arterial calcium stimulation test
Recommend ethanol ablation under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography
Refer to surgery for an extended distal pancreatectomy
Refer to surgery for a Whipple procedure
items.explanation:
81 ANSWER: B) Order a selective arterial calcium stimulation test

In patients with recurrent hypoglycemia 14 months after resection of a solitary insulinoma, the differential diagnoses are: (1) a “fractured” insulinoma (ie, residual tissue is left after attempted enucleation), (2) another insulinoma exists that was missed at the time of initial evaluation has now led to symptoms, or (3) metastatic disease has now progressed to the point that the patient has symptoms. Given these possibilities, initiating medical therapy with its attendant cost and comorbidity (fluid retention, nausea, bloating, hair growth, and breakthrough hypoglycemia) without attempting to regionalize the source of abnormal insulin secretion is inappropriate (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Considering the negative imaging studies obtained thus far, performing a selective arterial calcium stimulation test (Answer B) would be reasonable. Assuming that regionalization is congruent with the previous tumor location, it would make sense to proceed with surgical reexploration. On the basis of information

available in the vignette, there is no indication for an extended distal pancreatectomy (Answer D) or Whipple procedure (Answer E). Ethanol ablation under guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography (Answer C) would be indicated if this imaging modality could visualize residual tissue, but this is unlikely to be the case in the short interval since surgery.

Educational Objective:

Evaluate recurrent hypoglycemia after resection of an insulinoma.

Insulinoma

A 68-year-old man with a 7-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus is referred to you for progressively uncontrolled hyperglycemia. Diabetes was initially diagnosed when he presented to the hospital with chest pain and he subsequently underwent single coronary stent placement for obstructive coronary artery disease. He is currently taking metformin, 1000 mg twice daily, and glimepiride, 4 mg once daily in the morning. In the past 4 years, his fasting glucose levels have mostly ranged from 130 to 190 mg/dL (7.22-10.54 mmol/L). However, in the past few months, his fasting glucose levels have ranged from 240 to 260 mg/dL (13.32-14.43 mmol/L). He works on his farm for 5 to 6 hours daily. He has rare symptoms of hypoglycemia, most often after skipping lunch, when he feels weak and tired. He has had no severe hypoglycemia.

On physical examination, his blood pressure is 135/64 mm Hg and pulse rate is 57 beats/min. Weight is 152.5 lb (69.3 kg), and height is 66 in (167.7 cm) (BMI 24.6 kg/m²). He has no retinopathy. There are normal findings on cardiopulmonary and peripheral nerve examinations, normal peripheral pulses, and flat plantar foot calluses.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=10.6\% $(0.106)

Creatinine = 0.8 mg/dL (70.7 μmol/L)

Glomerular filtration rate = 92 mL/min per 1.73 m $ ^{2} $

Urinary albumin = 17 mg/g creatinine

Which treatment addition would be expected to lead to the best outcomes in this patient with respect to glycemic control?
options: Premeal rapid-acting insulin with titration instructions
A sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitor
A glucagonlike peptide 1 agonist
A thiazolidinedione
Basal insulin with titration instructions

A 55-year-old woman seeks your opinion for apparent episodes of hypoglycemia. She underwent Rouxen-Y gastric bypass 8 years ago for the treatment of medically complicated obesity. At that time, she had hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with 2 oral medications. The patient experienced diabetes remission within weeks of the procedure. Her preoperative weight was 300 lb (136.4 kg). The procedure and subsequent weight loss were uncomplicated, with nadir weight being reached approximately 14 months after surgery. She has regained some weight, and at today's visit she weighs 240 lb (109.1 kg). For the past 2 years, she has been troubled by symptoms of tachycardia, tremor, and diaphoresis that typically occur within an hour or two of food ingestion. These symptoms have progressively worsened. The patient reports that on one occasion, she required assistance after an episode of presyncope and altered mentation. A home reflectance glucose meter regularly reports glucose readings between 30 and 50 mg/dL (1.66 and 2.78 mmol/L) at the time symptoms occur. Before her visit with you, her primary care physician arranged a meal challenge; the patient was told to eat a meal that provokes her symptoms. She ate pancakes with maple syrup. The following results were obtained:

## 91 ANSWER: E) Referral to a dietician

91 A Just as the oral glucose tolerance test has no role in the evaluation of hypoglycemia, having a patient who has undergone Roux-en-Y gastric bypass consume a meal with calories in liquid form will most likely precipitate a constellation of symptoms often called “dumping” and low glucose values may be observed. This is a well-known effect of such surgery, and patients should be urged to adhere to dietary instructions after bariatric procedures. Up to 10% of healthy patients exhibit post-oral glucose tolerance test glucose concentrations less than 50 mg/dL without symptoms of neuroglycemia, and the same considerations apply after a patient has had Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. In the postprandial setting, one should be wary of applying fasting criteria for endogenous hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia given that insufficient time has elapsed to clear all secreted  $ \beta $-cell polypeptides from the peripheral circulation. Indeed, this is borne out by the normal results after a mixed meal that meets the criteria for reasonable nutrition after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, a surgical procedure that results in unregulated emptying of liquid from the stomach. In such circumstances, ingestion of a caloric liquid often produces vasomotor symptoms.

For a patient such as the one in this vignette, enlisting the services of a dietician (Answer E) is the best management step. The dietician should be experienced in the care of patients who have undergone bariatric surgery that can lead to dumping. Typically these patients do not have neuroglycopenia. It is important to note that this patient had postprandial blood glucose values between 60 and 70 mg/dL (3.33 and 3.88 mmol/L), but these values may be normal in this setting and there was no accompanying neuroglycopenia. Limited weight loss after Rouxen-Y gastric bypass is often an indicator of poor dietary counseling and/or poor dietary adherence.

Calcium arterial stimulation testing (Answer A) should not be considered. Abnormalities because normative values have not been established for patients after upper gastrointestinal surgery. Calcium arterial stimulation testing should not be undertaken until after a firm diagnosis of a hypoglycemic disorder has been made and surgical therapy is being considered. The poor long-term response to distal pancreatectomy in such situations has cooled enthusiasm for this procedure in such patients. Likewise, endoscopic ultrasonography (Answer B) would only be indicated if evidence of hypoglycemia after a standard mixed meal is present and the Whipple triad is fulfilled. This requires documentation of symptoms that are compatible with neuroglycopenia, which occur in the presence of hypoglycemia and are reversed with amelioration of hypoglycemia.

A 72-hour fast (Answer C) is unlikely to be helpful given that these symptoms usually occur after meals. Diagnostic imaging such as CT (Answer D) and ultrasonography should be performed only after hyperinsulinemia hypoglycemia is confirmed, and they have no role until the diagnosis is established.

#### Educational Objective:

Educate hypoglycemia that occurs after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass with a standardized solid mixed-meal challenge.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Late complications of bariatric surgical operations

#### Reference(s):

Cryer PE, Axelrod L, Grossman AB, et al; Endocrine Society. Evaluation and management of adult hypoglycemic disorders: an Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(3):709-728.

Vanderveen KA, Grant CS, Thompson GB, et al. Outcomes and quality of life after partial pancreatectomy for noninsulinoma pancreatogenous hypoglycemia from diffuse islet cell disease. Surgery. 2010;148(6):1237-1245.

items.question:
You are asked to evaluate a 72-year-old man with longstanding type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and coronary artery disease who has been admitted to the emergency department for hypoglycemia. The patient was hospitalized 2 months earlier with volume overload and electrolyte abnormalities attributable to end-stage renal disease. He received emergent hemodialysis at that time, and for the past 6 weeks he has been performing peritoneal dialysis at home with an icodextrin-containing dialysate.

Over the past month, the patient has contacted his physician multiple times because of consistent elevations in his self-monitored blood glucose readings. His prescribed insulin doses have been repeatedly increased over this period, with the most recent adjustment occurring several days ago. The patient is now taking insulin detemir at a dosage of 20 units once daily along with 8 units of insulin glulisine at each meal with additional amounts taken if the blood glucose concentration is greater than 150 mg/dL (>8.33 mmol/L). After the most recent increase in his insulin doses, his self-monitored blood glucose values have decreased from an average of 225 mg/dL (12.49 mmol/L) to approximately 175 mg/dL (9.71 mmol/L). This morning, the patient administered 20 units of insulin detemir and a total of 10 units of insulin glulisine before breakfast. Approximately 30 minutes later, emergency medical services were called because he was found to be unresponsive.

The patient is currently afebrile and is not tachycardic or hypoxemic. His blood pressure has improved from 82/44 mm Hg to 126/77 mm Hg with the administration of intravenous fluids. His peritoneal dialysis catheter is intact without surrounding erythema or drainage, and his abdomen is soft and nontender. When he arrived at the emergency department, his serum glucose concentration was 29 mg/dL (1.61 mmol/L). The patient's mental status is now dramatically improved after administration of a 50% dextrose solution.

Which of the following best explains this patient's severe hypoglycemia?
options: Inaccurate glucose meter data due to dialysate type
Decreased renal gluconeogenesis
Decreased renal insulin clearance
Sepsis due to bacterial peritonitis
Previously undetected adrenal insufficiency
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

A 36-year-old man with a 22-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus is referred to you for the management of his diabetes and its complications. He has hypertension and proteinuria, and he has been on antihypertensive therapy consisting of chlorthalidone, 25 mg daily, and lisinopril, 40 mg daily, for the last 5 years. He takes insulin glargine at bedtime and insulin lispro at set doses before meals. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level has ranged from 8.6% to 13.1% (0.086 to 0.131) over the past 5 years. An ophthalmologic examination 4 months ago showed bilateral, moderate nonproliferative retinopathy. He feels well and has no concerns.

On physical examination, his blood pressure is 162/91 mm Hg and heart rate is 77 beats/min. Height is 72 in (182.9 cm), and weight is 213 lb (96.8 kg) (BMI = 28.9 kg/m²). He appears well and has no cushingoid features. Findings on cardiovascular examination are normal with no edema, good peripheral pulses, and no carotid or renal bruits. On neurologic examination, he has reduced vibration sense and decreased sensation in both feet.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=9.8\% $ (0.098)

Serum urea nitrogen = 28 mg/dL (10.0 mmol/L)

Potassium = 5.4 mEq/L (5.4 mmol/L)

Carbon dioxide = 24 mEq/L (24 mmol/L)

Creatinine = 2.2 mg/dL (194.5 μmol/L)

Hematocrit = 34% (0.34)

The urinalysis shows 3+ protein and no white blood cells, red blood cells, or casts.

Which of the following will most likely slow the progression of diabetic nephropathy?

He tries to eat a low-fat diet, moderates his intake of carbohydrates (and tries to consume those that are high in fiber), and consumes an average of 4 fruit and vegetables. Fasting glucose concentrations range mostly between 140 and 180 mg/dL (7.77 and 9.99 mmol/L) and glucose concentrations after breakfast range between 200 and 260 mg/dL (11.10 and 14.43 mmol/L). He takes lisinopril, 10 mg daily, for hypertension. He does not smoke cigarettes. He has no chest or calf claudication pain. Findings on review of systems are otherwise normal.

On physical examination, height is 69 in (175.3 cm) and weight is 175 lb (79.5 kg) (BMI = 25.8 kg/m²). His blood pressure is 148/81 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 84 beats/min. There is no retinopathy on dilated eye examination no carotid or femoral bruits, normal findings on peripheral nerve examination, and normal pedal pulses.

Hepatic function, normal

A fibrate
A statin

His pretreatment hematocrit was 42% (0.42) and his prostate-specific antigen concentration was 1.2 ng/ml (1.2 μg/L). His prostate was symmetrically enlarged, nontender, without any focal nodule or induration. The patient has been on testosterone therapy for 5 months and feels well. He reports improved sexual function and energy. He is tolerating the testosterone solution without any skin irritation. He has no lower urinary tract symptoms. items.explanation:
## 102 ANSWER: C) A statin Hypertriglyceridemia is

102 Hypertriglyceridemia is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, although causality remains unclear. Triglyceride levels above 1000 mg/dL (>11.3 mmol/L) increase the risk of pancreatitis and should be aggressively treated with dietary and lifestyle interventions and usually concomitant pharmacologic therapy with fibrates, niacin, or fish oil.

Patients with diabetes mellitus who have hypertriglyceridemia should be screened for potentially treatable secondary causes such as hypothyroidism; nephrotic syndrome; obesity; alcohol use; and medication use including estrogens, glucocorticoids, cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide, noncardioselective  $ \beta $-adrenergic blockers, tamoxifen, phenothiazines, isotretinoin, and antiretroviral agents.

In patients with modest hypertriglyceridemia, the treatment focus should be nonpharmacologic and include weight loss in obese patients; regular aerobic exercise; and restriction of carbohydrates, especially food high in sugars. Among patients with diabetes, improved glycemic control effectively lowers triglyceride levels. In general, antihyperglycemic agents lower triglyceride levels. Insulin promotes the synthesis of lipoprotein lipase, which hydrolyzes triglycerides to free fatty acids. Intravenous insulin can effectively lower markedly elevated triglycerides. However, lowering triglycerides with prandial insulin has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular risk (thus, Answer E is incorrect).

Several cholesterol-lowering agents effectively lower triglycerides, including brates (18%-45%), niacin (17%-26%), and statins (5%-30%). High-dosage atorvastatin and rosuvastatin have the greatest triglyceride-lowering effect in the statin class. Niacin also raises HDL-cholesterol levels and has a modest LDL-lowering effect, but it has never been shown in monotherapy trials to reduce cardiovascular events rates or mortality among patients with diabetes (thus, Answer D is incorrect). Pharmacologic intervention of isolated hypertriglyceridemia with agents such as fibrates has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular event rates (thus, Answer B is incorrect). The Veterans Affairs High-Density Lipoprotein Intervention Trial (VA-HIT) showed a significant reduction in cardiovascular events among patients treated with gemfibrozil whose primary lipid abnormality was a low HDL-cholesterol level. Treated patients had a 6% increase in HDL cholesterol and a 31% lowering of triglycerides. In the Fenofibrate Intervention and Event Lowering in Diabetes (FIELD) trial, patients older than 50 years who had diabetes mellitus and were not taking lipid-lowering agents were treated with fenofibrate or placebo. After 5 years, treated patients had nonsignificantly lower rates of coronary events, and a nonsignificantly higher rate of coronary death and all-cause mortality. Combination therapy with a statin and fibrate has not been shown to improve cardiovascular risk reduction when compared with treatment with a statin alone (thus, Answer A is incorrect).

In addition to hypertriglyceridemia, this patient's cardiovascular risk factors include hypertension and albuminuria. The American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology cardiovascular risk calculators scores this patient's 10-year cardiovascular risk at 17.6%, well above the 7.5% risk cutoff recommended for initiation of statin therapy, even in individuals whose baseline LDL-cholesterol concentration is well below 100 mg/dL (2.59 mmol/L). Given the high cardiovascular risk in patients with diabetes, the 2013 American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology guidelines recommend that a moderate- or high-intensity statin be started in patients with diabetes who are between 40 and 75 years old and have an LDL-cholesterol concentration of 70 to 189 mg/dL (1.81-4.90 mmol/L) without clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (thus, Answer C is correct).

R

items.question:
A 20-year-old woman presents for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia. Her medical history includes cystic fibrosis that was diagnosed at age 10 months. Recently, she has begun to lose weight despite adhering to treatment of her exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Her pulmonary status appears to be stable at this visit and she reports no acute pulmonary symptoms.

On physical examination, she is a thin, well-appearing young woman in no distress. Height is 59 in (149.9 cm), and weight is 93 lb (37.7 kg) (BMI = 18.8 kg/m²). She is afebrile, blood pressure is 100/68 mm Hg, and puls rate is 100 beats/min. You detect a few scattered crackles bilaterally on lung examination and sinus tachycardia cardiac examination. Conjunctivae are pale. Her thyroid gland is normal, as are her neurologic reflexes. She has digital clubbing.

A random blood glucose measurement is 147 mg/dL (8.2 mmol/L).

Which of the following is the best diagnostic test?
options: Hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ measurement
75-g oral glucose tolerance test
Fructosamine measurement
Continuous glucose monitoring
Measurement of glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies
items.explanation:
## 106 ANSWER: B) 75-g oral glucose tolerance test Diseases

100 Diseases involving the pancreas affect insulin production from this gland and thereby cause diabetes mellitus. Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) is unique in that in addition to losing insulin-secreting  $ \beta $ cells, there is also loss of  $ \alpha $ cells, which secrete glucagon. Such patients are therefore at an increased risk for both spontaneous and treatment-induced hypoglycemia.

Cystic fibrosis occurs in approximately 1 in 2500 live births, and it is the most common autosomal recessive condition in white populations. Treatment advances have significantly improved patient survival, with a median survival age of 40 years for babies born in the 1990s. CFRD is a unique disease that has overlapping features with type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Affected patients typically develop this condition after the onset of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. In the initial stages, patients are able to maintain normal glucose tolerance because of a combination of increased hepatic glucose production and increased peripheral glucose use. Subsequently, patients develop impaired glucose tolerance and diabetes associated with decreased hepatic insulin sensitivity and reduced insulin production. The occurrence of CFRD increases by 4% to 9% annually as a person with cystic fibrosis ages. The prevalence is approximately 9% at age 10 years and 43% at age 30 years and older. A decline in nutritional status and pulmonary function can occur in patients 2 to 4 years before CFRD is diagnosed, so timely diagnosis and initiation of insulin therapy are pivotal in maintaining health. Because the onset of CFRD can be insidious, annual screening with standard 75-g oral glucose tolerance testing (Answer B) is recommended in patients with cystic fibrosis beginning at age 10 years. Standard oral glucose tolerance testing thresholds are used to diagnose both impaired glucose tolerance

and diabetes in this population. Timely diagnosis and initiation of insulin therapy improves nutritional status and may help maintain pulmonary health in patients with this challenging type of diabetes.

Type 1 diabetes is associated with the (Answer E) and this diagnosis is very unlikely in this patient. Studies have shown that continuous glucose monitoring (Answer D) may be a useful tool for early detection of hyperglycemia in patients with cystic fibrosis. However, it has not been proven to be a diagnostic tool in this setting. Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ measurement (Answer A) and fructosamine measurement (Answer C) provide data about average blood glucose values over the previous 3 months and the previous 3 weeks, respectively. Accuracy of these test results can be affected by anemia, decreased albumin levels, acute illness, and intermittent steroid use, which may all occur frequently in patients with cystic fibrosis. For these reasons, these tests cannot be used to diagnose CFRD.

#### Educational Objective:

Select the best test to diagnose cystic fibrosis-related diabetes and describe the pathophysiology of this condition.

items.question:
A 22-year-old woman who has been seeing you for the management of hypothyroidism returns to clinic for a scheduled appointment. The patient's younger sister recently received a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes mellitus. Your patient is aware that she may be at increased risk of developing type 1 diabetes herself and seeks your advice regarding screening for this condition. None of her other relatives are known to have type 1 diabetes; however, type 2 diabetes was diagnosed in her maternal grandfather at age 70 years.

The patient has no known medical problems other than a 2-year history of hypothyroidism, which is adequately managed with levothyroxine. She has no history of hospitalizations or surgical interventions and has received all recommended vaccinations. She has had no recent illnesses, and her review of systems is unremarkable. In particular, she notes no changes in her energy level, weight, or menses. She has had no vision changes, polydipsia, polyuria, or nocturia.

On physical examination, the patient is alert and appears well. Blood pressure is 104/72 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. Height is 64 in (162.6 cm), and weight is 115 lb (52.3 kg) (BMI = 19.7 kg/m²). She is normally developed and well nourished. Other than the finding of a slightly enlarged thyroid gland, her physical examination findings are unremarkable.

You explain that screening for type 1 diabetes may not identify all patients who will eventually develop the condition. The patient wishes to proceed with testing nonetheless.

Which of the following is the best next step?
options: Referral to a clinical research study that performs type 1 diabetes screening
Hemoglobin A $ _{1c} $ measurement at today's visit
Measurement of fasting glucose, insulin, and C-peptide levels
Screening for islet-cell cytoplasmic autoantibodies and glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies E
items.explanation:
107 ANSWER: A) Referral to a clinical research study that performs type 1 diabetes screening. This young woman has a sibling who has recently received a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes; thus, she is understandably concerned regarding her own likelihood of developing diabetes. In general, type 1 diabetes is diagnosed when patients present with acute symptoms of hyperglycemia and possibly diabetic ketoacidosis. There is much interest in predicting which individuals will develop type 1 diabetes with the aim of more closely monitoring at-risk patients and perhaps intervening to prevent or delay disease. However, clear guidelines for the screening of at-risk but asymptomatic individuals in routine clinical practice are not yet established. Because this patient has a nontwin sibling with a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes, her personal risk of developing the same condition is estimated to be approximately 10%.

The American Diabetes Association recommends that individuals at higher risk, including relatives of patients with type 1 diabetes, be referred for antibody testing and risk assessment in the context of a clinical research study (Answer A). This coordinated screening may enhance understanding of individual risk, provide education regarding diabetes symptoms, and permit close follow-up with perhaps an earlier diabetes diagnosis in those who do progress. Given the potential benefits of trial participation and the absence of firm guidelines for screening in clinical practice, enrollment in a clinical trial would be the preferred option for this young woman. Additional information regarding participation in such trials is available at www.diabetestrialnet.org.

Measurement of hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ (Answer B), although a reasonable screening test for type 2 diabetes, is not appropriate method to screen for type 1 diabetes. Promisingly, studies of individuals at high risk do suggest that type 1 diabetes may be predicted on the basis of a variety of parameters. In the Diabetes Prevention Trial of type 1 Diabetes (DPT-1) (a study of islet-cell autoantibody-positive relatives of patients with type 1 diabetes), use a risk score (DPTRS) derived from parameters including BMI, age, fasting C-peptide level, and postchallenge glucose and C-peptide levels appeared to predict type 1 diabetes in that population. However, measurement of its patient's fasting glucose, insulin, and C-peptide levels in the clinical setting (Answer C) is unlikely to be similarly meaningful. In the TrialNet Natural History Study of antibody-positive relatives of individuals with type 1 diabetes, generation of a risk score incorporating levels of multiple islet antibodies has shown promise in predicting diabetes risk. However, there are not yet clear guidelines for the screening of asymptomatic individuals via

autoantibody testing in clinical practice. Thus, in-clinic screening for either the antibody combinations of islet-cell cytoplasmic autoantibodies and glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies (Answer D) or insulinoma-2-associated autoantibodies and zinc transporter 8 autoantibodies (Answer E) would not be the preferred next steps. Continued engagement of high-risk individuals in clinical trials is the best means of refining clinical care in the future.

##### Educational Objective:

Provide guideline-based screening recommendations to patients at risk for type 1 diabetes mellitus.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Overview of medical care in adults with diabetes mellitus

items.question:
A 40-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with dysuria and dyspareunia. Two months ago, type 2 diabetes was diagnosed on the basis of a random blood glucose measurement of 210 mg/dL (11.66 mmol/L) and an elevated hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ measurement of 7.0% (0.070). Treatment with canagliflozin, 300 mg daily, was initiated. Other medications include lisinopril for hypertension and simvastatin for hypercholesterolemia. She uses an intrauterine device for contraception.

She has no symptomatic or documented hypoglycemia, but she does report mild polyuria, polydipsia, and nocturia. In addition, she has started experiencing pain and discomfort during sexual intercourse during the past week and has some discomfort with urine voiding.

On physical examination, she is an afebrile obese woman in no apparent distress. Weight is 190 lb (86.4 kg), and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 31.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 76 beats/min. Physical examination findings are normal.

A hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} $ measurement is 6.8% (0.068), and urine dipstick analysis shows a yellow and cloudy sample, 2+ positive glucose, negative protein, and 2+ leukocyte esterase.

In addition to a urine culture and treatment of the suspected urinary tract infection with antibiotics, which of the following additional steps would be appropriate?
options: Remove the intrauterine device
Reduce the canagliflozin dosage
Discontinue canagliflozin
Begin additional treatment for endometritis
Recommend no additional therapy at this time
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

items.question:
You are asked to evaluate a 74-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus who has been admitted to the hospital with hyperglycemia. She lives alone and is periodically visited by family members who live in a nearby town. The patient's brother brought her to the emergency department today after he found her to be confused and incontinent of urine. There was little food in her home and it is unclear whether she had been eating regularly or taking any medications for the management of hyperglycemia.

On physical examination, the patient is quite somnolent. She is afebrile. Blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg and pulse rate is 92 beats/min. Height is 66 in (167.6 cm), and weight is 185 lb (84.1 kg) (BMI = 29.9 kg/m^{2}). She has dry mucous membranes and skin tending is evident. Findings on examination of the chest and abdomen are normal. A comprehensive neurologic examination is limited by her somnolence. She is irritable when aroused but is without obvious focal neurologic deficit.

Laboratory test results:
Sodium = 134 mEq/L (134 mmol/L)
Potassium = 5.3 mEq/L (5.3 mmol/L)
Chloride = 106 mEq/L (106 mmol/L)
Serum bicarbonate = 18 mEq/L (18 mmol/L)
Serum urea nitrogen = 79 mg/dL (28.2 mmol/L)
Creatinine = 1.9 mg/dL (168.0 μmol/L)

Effective serum osmolality = 321.4 mOsm/kg (321.4 mmol/kg)

Arterial pH = 7.35

Glucose = 961 mg/dL (53.3 mmol/L)

Urinary ketones, small

Serum  $ \beta $-hydroxybutyrate = 2.8 mg/dL (269.0  $ \mu $mol/L)

##### Which of the following should be included in this patient's initial care plan?
options: 1.5 L of hypertonic saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 12 units per hour
1.5 L of isotonic saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 12 units per hour
1.5 L of isotonic saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 4 units per hour
1.5 L of 0.45% saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 12 units per hour
1.5 L of 0.45% saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 4 units per hour
items.explanation:
## 114 ANSWER: B) 1.5 L of isotonic saline over the next hour, and intravenous insulin infusion at 12 units per hour

This older patient with type 2 diabetes has developed clinically significant hyperglycemia and an altered mental status requiring hospitalization. She meets established criteria for the hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) because she has severe hyperglycemia (plasma glucose >600 mg/dL [>33.30 mmol/L]), hyperosmolality (effective serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg [>320 mmol/kg]), and dehydration without evidence of marked ketoacidosis. In HHS, the arterial pH is typically above 7.30, the serum bicarbonate is greater than 15 mEq/L (>15 mmol/L), and the anion gap is variable. If detected, only small amounts of ketones are present in the urine and serum. This differs from diabetic ketoacidosis in which hyperglycemia is associated with elevated serum and urinary ketones and metabolic acidosis. In contrast to what is observed in diabetic ketoacidosis, endogenous insulin levels in HHS are generally high enough to prevent lipolysis and the generation of ketone bodies but are not high enough to facilitate glucose uptake into insulin-sensitive tissues.

HHS is estimated to comprise approximately 1% of all diabetes-related hospital admissions, and it is most commonly precipitated by inadequate or omitted insulin dosing or an infectious process. However, HHS may also be associated with conditions such as cerebrovascular or cardiovascular events, pancreatitis, or the use of medications such as glucocorticoids or thiazide diuretics. Older persons with type 2 diabetes are at greatest risk for the development of HHS because they are more likely to become severely dehydrated due to immobilization or an impaired thirst mechanism. The identification and appropriate management of HHS is essential. Even in developed countries, the associated mortality rate is estimated to be between 5% and 20% and is largely attributable to underlying precipitating conditions.

Individuals with HHS are very dehydrated, with estimated total water deficits at presentation typically between 8 and 10 L. Fluid repletion alone will greatly reduce hyperglycemia and reduce insulin resistance, and it represents a critical component of care. As is the case with this patient, the measured serum sodium is often low; however, this is misleading because it is due to the hyperglycemia-induced osmotic flux of water from the intracellular space to the extracellular space. This patient is in fact hypernatremic when the measured sodium level is corrected for the plasma glucose. Thus, the administration of hypertonic saline (Answer A) is not indicated and, in fact, might worsen her hyperosmolarity and hypernatremia. Initial fluid therapy in HHS should include isotonic saline administered at a rate of 15 to 20 mL/kg (or 1 to 1.5 L) over the first hour. Thereafter, hyponatremic patients should be administered isotonic saline at a rate of 250 to 500 mL/h, while patients with normal to high sodium levels should receive 0.45% saline at that rate. Administration of 0.45% saline at this time (Answers D and E) does not represent appropriate initial fluid resuscitation. Although not highlighted in this case, patients with HHS often have an initially high potassium level due to the shift of potassium into the extracellular space but are in fact total-body potassium depleted. Potassium replacement should begin when the serum potassium level falls below the upper limit of the normal range and should generally be maintained between 4 and 5 mEq/L (4 and 5 mmol/L).

HHS should also be managed via a low-dosage insulin infusion protocol. Administration of an initial 0.1 unit/kg body weight intravenous insulin bolus followed by 0.1 unit/kg per hour continuous insulin infusion is recommended; alternatively, administration of a continuous intravenous insulin infusion at a rate of 0.14 unit/kg body weight per hour is also appropriate. Use of such low-dosage insulin protocols generally decr

items.question:
A 28-year-old woman with a 12-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus is referred to you for management of her diabetes in the setting of preconception planning. She is using continuous subcutaneous infusion of insulin. She usually experiences hypoglycemia twice a month, has good awareness of hypoglycemic symptoms, and has not required outside assistance to manage these episodes. She has never used glucagon. The patient is currently taking an oral contraceptive and vitamin supplementation. She is not taking any other medication. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable.

On physical examination, she has a soft ejection systolic murmur, but there is no ankle edema. There is no atrophy of subcutaneous fat and no bruises or striae. Weight is 160 lb (72.7 kg), and height is 69 in (175.3 cm) (BMI = 23.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and there are no postural changes in blood pressure. Sensation to vibration, monofilament, and temperature is intact. Fundoscopy reveals the presence of microaneurysms in both eyes.

Results of laboratory tests ordered by her primary care physician (sample drawn at 8 AM, fasting):

 $$ \mathrm{Hemoglobin}=11.2\mathrm{g/dL}(112\mathrm{g/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{M C V}=79\mathrm{\mu m}^{3}(79\mathrm{f L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{White~blood~cell~count}=5700/\mu\mathrm{L}(5.7\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Platelet\ count}=208\times10^{3}/\mu\mathrm{L}(208\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Glucose}=114\mathrm{mg}/\mathrm{dL}(6.33\mathrm{mmol}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Sodium}=138\mathrm{m E q/L}(138\mathrm{~mmol/L}) $$ 

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=6.8\% $ (0.068)

 $$ \mathrm{P o t a s s i u m=4.5~m E q/L~(4.5~m m o l/L)} $$ 

 $$ Creatinine=0.8\ mg/dL(70.7\ \mu mol/L) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{TSH}=2.2\,\mathrm{mIU/L} $$ 

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 18 ng/mL (44.9 nmol/L)

Albumin-to-creatinine ratio = 5 µg/mg

In addition to optimizing her glycemic control, measuring which of the following is the most important next management step?
options: TPO antibodies
Tissue transglutaminase antibodies
Iothalamate clearance
Bone mineral density
Glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies
items.explanation:
## 119 ANSWER: B) Tissue transglutaminase antibodies

119 Celiac disease occurs with increased frequency in persons with type 1 diabetes mellitus and in such instances, malabsorption can result in deficiency of iron, folate, and fat-soluble vitamins such as vitamin D. These deficiencies could have adverse effects on maternal and fetal health (especially with respect to folate deficiency and fetal neural tube defects). The presence of microcytosis in a patient with type 1 diabetes and mild anemia should raise the possibility of celiac disease. Measuring tissue transglutaminase antibodies (Answer B) is recognized as an appropriate screening tool in these circumstances and this is the next step for this patient.

On the basis of her physical examination and current laboratory results, measurement of TPO antibodies (Answer A) as a marker of thyroid autoimmunity is unlikely to guide management. She is euthyroid, and with a normal TSH level, the recommendation would be to follow TSH.

Given the patient's unremarkable history, normal physical examination findings, normal serum creatinine concentration, and no microalbuminuria, detailed measures of renal function, such as iothalamate clearance (Answer C), are not indicated.

Because of the patient's age, absence of fracture history, and established diagnosis of diabetes treated with insulin, measurement of bone mineral density (Answer D) or glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies (Answer E) is unlikely to be helpful in her preconception management. In light of the patient's low 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentration, replacement therapy is indicated.

##### Educational Objective:

Suspect celiac disease in a patient with type 1 diabetes mellitus, microcytosis, and mild anemia.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Pathogenesis, epidemiology, and clinical manifestations of celiac disease in adults

Reference(s):

Schuppan D, Hahn EG. Celiac disease and its link to type 1 diabetes mellitus. J Pediatr Endocrinol Metab. 2001;14(Suppl 1):597-605.

Cronin CC, Feighery A, Ferriss Jb, Liddy C, Shanahan F, Feighery C. High prevalence of celiac disease among patients with insulin-dependent (type I) diabetes mellitus. Am J Gastroenterol. 1997;92(12):2210-2212.
